question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department’s efforts to develop citizen energy communities, which contribute to renewable energy production, as referenced in her Ministerial Statement on 2 March 2026.
answer_text: The development of citizen energy communities is an important part of my Department’s Energy Strategy and our commitment to putting people at the heart of the energy transition. As I set out in my Ministerial Statement on 2 March 2026, here in the North we have taken a leadership role in the BritishIrish Council Energy Work Sector on the theme of Public Acceptance. This includes supporting opportunities for citizens and communities to participate directly in local energy generation and consumption, increasing renewable energy use while delivering social and economic benefits at a local level.  Following the BritishIrish Council Energy Ministerial meeting in February, my officials are actively working with colleagues across the member administrations to share learning and best practice on community and citizenled energy initiatives, and to explore how barriers to delivery can be addressed. My Department is considering how citizen energy communities can support the wider delivery of the Energy Strategy, including reducing reliance on fossil fuels, tackling energy poverty and supporting a just transition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the recent reduction in the inpatient and day case waiting list reflects increased treatment activity or patient removal following waiting list validation.
answer_text: While Health and Social Care Trusts continue to undertake validation of their waiting lists to ensure they reflect those patients who are clinically fit and who still require their procedure, my Department is working in parallel to deliver additional HSC activity. From April 2025 to January 2026 there has been an additional 118,000 consultations, diagnostic tests and treatments delivered by our Trusts. Funding has also been provided to support the ongoing delivery of Primary Care Elective Services. This investment has enabled the provision of approximately 15,300 facetoface and remote consultations across the routine elective pathways. The expansion of HSC capacity has been complemented with the new waiting list reimbursement scheme and the continued use of inhouse and independent sector initiatives. This capacity has enabled the delivery of a further 119,000 consultations, diagnostic tests and treatments. These initiatives have helped reduce the waiting times for a range of common conditions including primary hip and primary knee replacements where approximately 1,000 patients have either had their joint surgery or have a confirmed date for their joint surgery.Working with Professor Mark Taylor, my Department has made significant progress to reduce waiting times. Between March 2025 and February 2026, there has been a 50% reduction in the number of patients projected to be waiting over four years for an outpatient appointment by 31 March 2026 and there has been a 62% reduction in patients waiting for inpatient and day case treatment. In terms of the named procedures detailed in the Elective Care Framework there has been an 89% reduction in patients waiting over four years.To ensure that the progress which has been made against the PfG target this year is not reversed, I need the Executive to deliver on the commitment within the 2026/27 allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the importance of retained fire fighters to the functioning of the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS), to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Education in relation to changing Department of Education guidance to allow teachers to take paid leave to attend mandatory NIFRS training as an on-call firefighter, since the start of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My department remains committed to supporting the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) in maintaining a sustainable and resilient retained workforce. Whilst NIFRS has no restrictions on teachers serving as retained officers, they do not normally apply for oncall duties that would conflict with their primary employment. I understand the ability to attend mandatory training is important for retention and operational readiness. NIFRS seeks to be as flexible as possible in training retained firefighters whilst balancing their primary jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration given to updating Circular 1993/31 regarding Teachers' Leave of Absence Exceeding Three Working Days, to allow teachers to take paid leave to attend mandatory Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service training, as an on-call firefighter.
answer_text: Teachers’ Pay and Conditions of Service are negotiated and agreed through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which is made up of employing authorities, employer representatives, representatives of the five main teaching unions as well as Departmental representatives.  There is no proposal to provide paid leave for teachers who are also part-time fire fighters to attend mandatory Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service training.I am fully supportive of the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service but my first priority is the education of pupils in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons why trees along Dundrum Road, Dundrum, have been cut down.
answer_text: Improvements to pedestrian and cycling infrastructure between Clough and Dundrum have been delivered to enhance safety and accessibility along the Dundrum Road. As part of these works, approximately 45 trees were unavoidably removed. My Department recognises the importance of trees within the local environment. While it is not always feasible to provide replacement planting within the boundaries of every scheme, funding was previously provided to Newry Mourne and Down District Council to support tree planting in alternative locations. Following completion of the first phase of the Clough to Dundrum Active Travel Route, a feasibility study examined a range of options to complete the route. The preferred option was selected on the basis of deliverability and value for money, enabling the provision of a highquality active travel facility along the A2. Engagement with stakeholders, landowners and local elected representatives commenced in 2023 and continued through to November 2025, including further engagement associated with a redesign of a short section adjacent to Dundrum GAC.Surface water drainage along the A2 is managed through road gullies that connect into the NI Water drainage network, which discharges to the sea at three locations. During construction, an NI Water manhole was identified and cleared, which is expected to improve drainage performance along this section of the route.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any immediate actions the Education Authority plan to take to restore safe conditions for learning and dining at St Francis' Primary School, Drumaroad, following the recent environmental health inspection that resulted in the condemnation of the school's canteen.
answer_text: The Education Authority has scoped the works required to the dining mobile unitand these works are expected to be delivered within the next 10–14 days as urgent maintenance business. In the interim, packed lunches are being provided to the school. Planning approval has been received for a scheme to provide a purpose-built multi-purpose hall at the school. This will include school meals accommodation and would replace the current dining mobile unit. The design team is currently preparing a scope for archaeological works, which must be undertaken prior to construction. A decision to release the project to construction tender will be taken based on the prioritisation and the budget available at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount she has allocated to (i) structural road maintenance; and (ii) essential routine road maintenance activities, in 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department’s spend on structural road maintenance and essential routine road maintenance activities in 2024-25 are detailed in the table below.Category of spend£mCapital Structural Maintenance107.4Resource Structural Maintenance28.4Routine Maintenance24.1Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments. Resource Structural Maintenance includes patching of the carriageway and footways. Routine Maintenance includes activities such grass cutting, tree maintenance and gully emptying.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) percentage spend; and (ii) absolute spend, on road defect repairs carried out by her Department versus external contractors, in 2025.
answer_text: My Department’s spend in delivering road structural maintenance, which it carried out, was £20.4m in 2024-25. This accounted for 15% of the total structural maintenance spend of £135.8m in 2024-25, with the remaining delivery through external contractors.  Structural Maintenance is the collective term for expenditure on resurfacing, surface dressing, patching on both carriageway and footways, structural drainage and structural stability of the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of road defects repaired by (i) her Department; and (ii) external contractors, in 2025.
answer_text: My Department carries out a range of road maintenance works which are categorised by activity such as patching or environmental maintenance and funding is recorded by this means.We also maintain a large and varied road network of both rural and urban roads, ranging from low trafficked routes to high speed ‘A’ Class roads and Dual Carriageways. There are differing requirements which are dependent on the types of roads, particularly between rural and urban and it is also the case that high-speed roads require additional signage, with increased costs. Therefore, a cost per mile of road maintenance works cannot be derived in a meaningful way.My Department recorded approximately 80,000 defect repairs in 2024/25 of which 61.8% were repaired by my Department’s internal contractor and 38.2% by an externally appointed contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost per mile of road maintenance works carried out by (i) by her Department; and (ii) external contractors, in 2025.
answer_text: My Department carries out a range of road maintenance works which are categorised by activity such as patching or environmental maintenance and funding is recorded by this means.We also maintain a large and varied road network of both rural and urban roads, ranging from low trafficked routes to high speed ‘A’ Class roads and Dual Carriageways. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether traffic cones, that are often found placed in potholes on roads across Northern Ireland, are being placed there by DFI staff; and (ii) for her assessment of the road safety implications of such objects being placed on roads, for the purpose of highlighting potholes.
answer_text: When my Department becomes aware of a severe pothole needing urgent attention, arrangements are made to deliver a permanent repair within the timescales set out in DEM 160 – Road Maintenance Standards for Safety.Where a permanent repair cannot be achieved within an acceptable timescale, a temporary repair may be delivered and followed up with a permanent repair.In exceptional cases, the usage of signage and cones to ensure the safety of road users may be deployed, however this would be in line with our signage policy and would not take the form of a single cone within the pothole.While I understand that there is a desire by members of the public to highlight potholes to other road users, the placing of cones in potholes other than in line with our signage policy may cause a hazard in itself and pose a greater risk to safety than the original defect, especially in unlit areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent by her Department on temporary road repairs, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department always seeks to undertake a first time permanent repair within the target repair times, however it may occasionally be necessary to carry out an initial repair in temporary material in advance of planning a separate visit for a permanent repair, for example on a busy junction.  In addition, out of hours responses may require an initial repair in temporary material in advance of a permanent repair.  The cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for patching. This term is applied to small scale works necessary to keep roads in a satisfactory state of repair, including infilling potholes but also the repair of small areas affected by cracking or settlement, as such it is not possible to provide a breakdown of the cost attributable solely to pothole repairs, including those of a temporary nature.I have recently allocated an additional £7.85 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. This will be used to deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need across the north. This work will commence without delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is considering to strengthen consumer protections in relation to gift vouchers, in particular with regards to expiry dates.
answer_text: I have no plans at this time to bring forward legislation to regulate the issuance of gift cards.  A small proportion of gift vouchers and cards are already regulated, and consumers purchasing these benefit from greater protection than when purchasing gift cards issued by individual retailers or service providers. These are “open loop” electronic cards, which can be used in a wide range of retailers. These gift cards and vouchers are subject to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 and require the issuer to safeguard all funds received in exchange for the electronic card issued.To introduce further statutory protection of gift cards would require complex consideration. Gift cards are frequently purchased as gifts and in this circumstance it is likely that the purchaser would benefit from any protection, rather than the gift card recipient.  Consumers who purchase gift cards exceeding £100 can currently seek redress on retailer insolvency by pursuing a refund from their credit card provider, who is jointly liable under section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act 1974. If the gift card value is below £100, consumers may be able to make a claim under chargeback rules of certain credit or debit card providers. Concerned consumers can seek free advice on gift card and voucher purchases from my Department’s Trading Standards Service consumer helpline on 0300 123 62 62 or by email to: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/contact-consumerline-make-complaint-or-ask-adviceIt is generally good advice for consumers to check the terms written on the voucher, particularly expiry dates, inactivity fees and restrictions on whether you can spend in store or online.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department monitors the lifespan of pothole repairs, carried out by road maintenance contractors on its behalf.
answer_text: I recently launched our new Road Maintenance Strategy, which is now open for public consultation. While the main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, the provision of a ground-breaking full digital survey of the North’s entire road network will provide a level of detail that will allow my officials to take data-driven, efficient decisions and interventions. While the quality of repairs is monitored as part of routine highway inspections, the new Road Maintenance Strategy will be a major advance in the assessment of the condition and durability of repairs on the network as well as the performance of reinstatements associated with utility openings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any contractual obligations in place, in relation to the quality and durability of (i) temporary; and) ii) long-term, repairs carried out by road maintenance contractors on her Department's behalf.
answer_text: Temporary and long-term repairs, as distinct from structural resurfacing, are mainly delivered through Term Contracts for Small-Scale and Responsive Works. The quality and durability requirements for these repairs are set out in the Specification for Highway Works, alongside contract-specific amendments.Durability is ensured through compliance with the specification, which requires the use of approved materials meeting relevant Standards, adherence to defined construction and workmanship requirements, and the application of inspection and testing regimes.Temporary repairs are intended to address immediate safety hazards until a permanent repair is completed. They are not designed to provide long-term durability, and the contract does not specify related time periods. Contractors must, however, ensure temporary repairs remain safe and effective for their short-term purpose.Permanent repairs are covered by a 52-week defect correction period, during which contractors must rectify any defects arising from their materials or workmanship.The forthcoming Road Maintenance Strategy is expected to improve how the condition and durability of repairs across the network are monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to ensure that emails from constituents and local councillors are processed in a timely manner.
answer_text: My Department is committed to responding to all queries submitted to it in a timely manner and my officials process emails from constituents and local councillors in line with The DfI Customer Charter. This can be found here:   https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfi-customer-charterThe Customer Charter outlines the different ways to contact my Department and sets service standards the public can expect when making contact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support he can provide to Ardmore Special School, given the school's increase in numbers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The support team within the Special Schools’ Service has developed strong links with Ardmore school via a Designated Support Officer. This continued support relates to issues such as accommodation, resourcing, training and specific pupil provision. The support officer works closely with colleagues in the Education Authority’s Education Directorate to provide ongoing support to Ardmore’s senior management team. The Special Schools’ Service has and will continue to support Ardmore House School to ensure that it has an adequate staffing and management complement that meets the needs of a growing school.  For the current academic year, the EA has funded an additional 3.4 teachers, 2 classroom assistants and a new Social Behaviour and Emotional Well Being Intervention Officer.  Furthermore, the school’s management structure has been bolstered with additional non-teaching vice principal allocation and 3 additional management allowances.  The school has a designated budgetary support officer who offers continuous support and training to the principal on the various financial reporting responsibilities.

The EA has confirmed that the construction tender seeking contractor submissions for the proposed work issued in January 2026. The tender return date is currently scheduled for early February 2026, subject to any received clarifications. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of a recent road traffic incident involving a child outside Assumption Grammar School, Ballynahinch and concerns around road safety in the town, for an update on the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass, including a timeline for its completion.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users and we are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.I am aware of the recent incident and would like to send my well wishes to the young girl and her family and to wish her a speedy recovery.  I have asked my officials to review the existing signage and road markings to ensure they are appropriate for the area, and we will work with the PSNI with regard to any associated investigation into the incident.Concerns regarding driver behaviour can also be reported to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.On 12 March 2025, I announced the progression of the Ballynahinch Bypass Scheme which would be delivered sequentially to the A1 Junctions project.Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I have taken time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery.While we await the ruling from the Court Appeal, my officials continue to work on the next steps in relation to the project and I remain committed to the delivery of this vitally important infrastructure scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a timeline for the reopening of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre.
answer_text: Sport NI has primary responsibility for the management of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre, making this an operational matter for the organisation. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of Sport NI who has provided me with the following response.		“Stakeholders are currently being contacted by the Fathom team and invited to participate in Stakeholder Consultation sessions taking place during the course of February 2026.		It is expected that preferred options for a target operating model will be presented to Sport NI for consideration before the end of the financial year (March 2026).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the systems review regarding Tollymore National Outdoor Centre.
answer_text: Sport NI has primary responsibility for the management of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre, making this an operational matter for the organisation. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of Sport NI who has provided me with the following response.“Sport NI has appointed a specialist advisory firm, Fathom Consultancy Solutions (Fathom), to support the Strategic Review of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre (TNOC). The project commenced in December 2025.The Strategic Review is aimed at finding a more financially sustainable operating model for the publicly owned centre. In the last financial year of its operation, Tollymore National Outdoor Centre cost over £1.3million to run, versus an income of around £250,000.The Strategic Review will examine how the specialist needs of the region’s outdoor sector could be balanced with improved utilisation of the centre’s outdoor recreation facilities by the wider community and visitors; and will examine a range of possible operating models.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the absence of a full reopening, to detail any consideration his Department has given to allowing access to Tollymore National Outdoor Centre's assets, such as the climbing wall and swimming pool, for use by qualified private individuals and group operators.
answer_text: Sport NI has primary responsibility for the management of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre, making this an operational matter for the organisation. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of Sport NI who has provided me with the following response. “Sport NI have been considering interim arrangements in respect of some elements of TNOC. However, the current priority is engaging with the sector during the consultation phase. Sport NI will revisit this matter once the consultation exercise is complete.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; (ii) maximum; and (iii) minimum, ambulance response times, for each category of call, in each month of 2025, broken down by (a) District Electoral Area in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area; and (a) local council area.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has in relation to footpaths in rural areas, given her active travel commitments.
answer_text: Active travel improvements taken forward by my Department are for road safety reasons, and to encourage modal shift.Improvements which are prioritised on the basis of road safety are undertaken in line with the Roads Policy and Procedure Guide (RSPPG) EO32.  Pedestrian enhancement measures provided under this policy include the installation of pedestrian crossings, missing link footways and provision of dropped kerbs.Active travel improvements are typically prioritised in line with delivery plans developed with the aim of creating well connected pedestrian and cycling networks.Since the introduction of the Climate Change Act, the main focus of my Department, with respect to encouraging more people to walk, wheel or cycle, has concentrated on developing the Active Travel Delivery Plan and enhancing my department’s design guidance for the delivery of active travel schemes. Consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan demonstrated the need to take a different approach when prioritising high-quality active travel infrastructure in rural areas to that applied in an urban context. Once work on the Active Trave Delivery Plan is complete, officials will consider how best to prioritise active travel investment to maximise benefits in rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any speed reduction measures her Department will consider along Loughinisland Road, Loughinisland.
answer_text: Regrettably, a previous assessment against the competing priorities in the Newry Mourne and Down area did not rank the proposal for the extension of the footways at this location highly enough for inclusion in a works programme.  My officials will undertake a fresh assessment of the location to determine if any new factors need to be taken into consideration.  Engagement with landowners would only take place if the scheme was to be prioritised within my Department’s current work programme.My Department’s policy for setting speed limits is designed to promote consistency for motorists, ensuring that similar roads are subject to similar speed restrictions. In determining the appropriate limit, we take into account a range of factors, including the nature and characteristics of the road, prevailing traffic speeds, the extent of frontage development, and road traffic collision statistics, which are routinely monitored using data from the PSNI.A previous assessment of the current 30mph speed limit considered it appropriate, and there are no plans for its extension at this time.

answer_text: Regrettably, a previous assessment against the competing priorities in the Newry Mourne and Down area did not rank the proposal for the extension of the footways at this location highly enough for inclusion in a works programme.  My officials will undertake a fresh assessment of the location to determine if any new factors need to be taken into consideration.  Engagement with landowners would only take place if the scheme was to be prioritised within my Department’s current work programme.My Department’s policy for setting speed limits is designed to promote consistency for motorists, ensuring that similar roads are subject to similar speed restrictions. In determining the appropriate limit, we take into account a range of factors, including the nature and characteristics of the road, prevailing traffic speeds, the extent of frontage development, and road traffic collision statistics, which are routinely monitored using data from the PSNI.A previous assessment of the current 30mph speed limit considered it appropriate, and there are no plans for its extension at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her planning decision on the Hightown Incinerator.
answer_text: The applicant has informed my officials of their intention to submitted further information to address issues raised by statutory consultees following a consultation exercise which was completed in autumn 2025. Once this information is received, further re-consultation and re-advertisement will be undertaken by my planning officials as part of the statutory process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that active travel spending can be used to fund street lighting under her Department's new accounting policy, whether she will consider extending street lighting in areas where an interest is shown.
answer_text: My Department’s current policy for the provision of street lighting in rural locations considers:The density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant nighttime use; and, Road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.I am conscious that street lighting can contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women travelling at night. This is an important consideration when officials are developing proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling infrastructure to key destinations that have greatest potential for modal shift, such as routes to train stations or Park & Ride sites on the edge of towns.However, requests for lighting must be balanced against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside, the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and flora, and our desire to reduce the carbon footprint and electricity costs associated with our infrastructure. While there are no plans to change the street lighting policy at this time, my Department will consider the merit of providing lighting as part of the design process for prioritised active travel schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the benefits of changes made to land eligibility under the Farm Sustainability Payment for nature, the environment and farm businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will introduce grants for solar panels on domestic properties.
answer_text: Financial support is not currently available for homeowners who wish to install solar panels. The Windsor Framework enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. This means that people here can apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials such as solar panels.Feedback from the low carbon heat consultation in 2025 suggested that combining solar panels with a heat pump could reduce overall operating costs. This option is being considered as we take forward the development of the low carbon heat and energy efficiency support scheme in 2026. In the meantime, households may still find solar panels or solar with battery storage, a worthwhile investment offering long-term savings, even in the absence of grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce grants for electric vehicle charge points for homeowners, other than those living in flats.
answer_text: While DfI does not have any grants available for the installation of EV charging, the UK Government offers a range of grants (available in Northern Ireland) towards EV Infrastructure, including:	Electric vehicle charge point grant for renters and flat owners;	Electric vehicle charge point grant for households with on-street parking;	Workplace Charging Scheme;	Electric vehicle charge points and infrastructure grant for landlords;	Electric vehicle infrastructure grant for staff and fleets; and	Workplace Charging Scheme for state-funded education institutions.Further detail on available grants  can be found online at: Electric vehicle chargepoint grants - GOV.UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail which body currently holds responsibility for coordinating support for a vulnerable young person unable to attend education settings due to mental health and trauma-related needs.
answer_text: Article 86(1) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 places a statutory duty on the Education Authority to make arrangements for the provision of suitable education at school or otherwise than at school for those children of compulsory school age who by reason of illness, expulsion from school or otherwise, may not for any period receive suitable education unless such arrangements are made for them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which body holds responsibility for coordinating support for a vulnerable young person unable to attend education due to mental health and trauma-related needs.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is the responsible authority for coordinating support for any young person who is unable to attend education due to mental health or trauma related needs.My Department offers support to colleagues working in the education setting through the Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools (EWTS) project, a health-led initiative delivered within the joint DE/Department of Health, Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education framework. EWTS is offered to all post primary and EOTAS (Education Other Than At School) settings on a cohort basis and promotes an integrated health-informed approach, to include strengthening the emotional readiness of pupils whose mental health impacts their ability to engage in learning.This health-led contribution supports DE’s statutory responsibilities by helping to equip school staff to support vulnerable young people to access education in a way and at a pace that aligns with their individual wellbeing needs.My Department also offers support directly to young people through the Child & Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) provision, which is available in all five HSC Trusts.  CAMHS adhere to the Regional CAMHS Integrated Care Pathway - Working together: A pathway for Children and Young People through CAMHS (which encompasses the stepped care model) and aims to ensure the delivery of health care services for children by prioritising the least intensive yet most effective treatment first. EWTS works directly with school staff to build their understanding of thresholds and pathways to specialist support, including CAMHS and when referrals may be appropriate. As such children or young people referred for specialist/targeted intervention to CAMHS may have and will continue to have significant support being provided through multi-agency involvement whilst waiting to access CAMHS or indeed following and during care and treatment with CAMHS.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 37250/22-27, to detail any engagement she has had with the Utility Regulator, either in advance of or following her policy decision to deliver fairer grid connection charges, in relation to the Utility Regulator's willingness to consider additional charges for electricity consumers in future price controls.
answer_text: My Department and the Utility Regulator collaborated significantly in the development of the Fairer Grid Connection costs policy. My Department continues to work with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to bring this change through as quickly as possible, with change expected to take effect in the first half of 2026.		Connections made during the price control period will be funded through the RP7 Price Control Determination. Considerations in respect of future price controls will be for the Utility Regulator to determine at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 37250/22-27, whether her response means that the policy change to deliver fairer grid connection charges, will not be implemented within NIE Networks’ RP7 price control.
answer_text: Connections made during the price control period will be funded through the RP7 Price Control Determination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure with reference to her plans to introduce legislation to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking, to detail any additional actions she is taking to tackle situations where cars are parked in such a way that other road users' sightlines are blocked and roads are left too narrow for delivery vans and bin lorries to pass.
answer_text: The proposed ban on parking a vehicle wholly on the footway, parking across dropped kerbs, and double parking represents the most practical and effective way to address inconsiderate pavement parking at this stage, considering feasibility, enforcement considerations and legislative vires. My intention is to introduce these changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to the legislative process.My Department works collaboratively with others to address particular problem areas that are brought to our attention. For example, my Department has worked with Belfast City Council and the PSNI on the “Park with us in mind” initiative which targeted hotspots where council waste collection vehicles were often unable to gain access to areas due to poor, inconsiderate or illegal parking.  While this joint approach has helped alleviate issues in the short term, the new legislation will provide a more consistent and robust framework for tackling inconsiderate pavement parking across the North.These measures together with the already-introduced Article 8 of the Footpaths (Prohibition of Parking) Order 2023, will further support efforts to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, making streets safer and more accessible, particularly for disabled people, older citizens and those with prams.My Department currently has the authority to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) where a vehicle is observed parked on a legislated restriction, such as double yellow lines. Enforcement schedules remain flexible and, where resources permit, can be adjusted. If additional enforcement is required, my parking enforcement team can be contacted at Parking.Request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.However, my Department cannot take enforcement action where a vehicle is parked on a pavement causing an obstruction, but no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway. In such cases, responsibility lies with the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the amount NIE Networks will be able to raise per annum, from additional charges added to (i) domestic; and (ii) commercial customers' electricity bills, as a result of her decision to socialise grid connection costs.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator has confirmed that the policy change to deliver fairer grid connection charges, does not constitute a reopening of NIE Networks’ RP7 price control determinisation and therefore there will be no additional NIE Networks’ charges for electricity consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, with reference to findings from a recent study of untreated water samples from five waterways in Northern Ireland, conducted by the Agri-food and Biosciences Institute and Imperial College London, to detail any treatment that is carried out to remove traces of pharmaceuticals and illicit drugs from water supplied as drinking water.
answer_text: The treatment of the drinking water supply is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the information requested.NI Water has advised that it applies the drinking water safety planning approach in assessing the risks for contaminants in the raw water sources used for the abstraction at water treatment works. NI Water has multibarrier treatment processes in place at water treatment works to remove contaminants and reduce the risk to the treated drinking water supply to consumers. NI Water has advised that studies on drinking water treatment for the removal of pharmaceuticals show that water treatment processes such as ozonation and activated carbon, which are treatment processes undertaken by NI Water, are effective for the removal of pharmaceuticals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of incidents where other emergency services, such as the PSNI, Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service, Mourne Mountain Rescue Service, or the Coastguard, have responded to an emergency because an ambulance was unavailable, in each of the last five years, broken down by the responding emergency service.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) does not collect data in a format that would allow my Department to quantify occasions where another emergency service responded because an ambulance was unavailable. To clarify, all incidents requiring clinical support from NIAS do receive an ambulance response, although system pressures can occasionally result in delays.Other emergency services such as the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS), the Coastguard, and specialist voluntary organisations like Mourne Mountain Rescue, often attend incidents because the nature of the situation necessitates a multi-agency approach. Examples include road traffic collisions, rescues, hazardous environments, and water-related incidents.In some circumstances, the PSNI has transported patients to hospital, particularly during mental health crises or when immediate safety concerns arise. This reflects broader system pressures and the need for collaborative responses, rather than a formal substitution for ambulance services.NIFRS currently only records instances in which they mobilise to provide support to NIAS at an incident. NIFRS does not maintain records of mobilisations that occur when NIAS has declared they are unavailable to attend.NIAS continues to work with health and social care partners to improve response times and resilience. This includes investment in additional staff, enhanced triage systems, and collaborative initiatives with other emergency services to ensure patient safety during periods of high demand.I remain committed to strengthening emergency care provision and ensuring that patients receive timely and appropriate support. While multi-agency responses are sometimes necessary, my Department’s focus is on improving ambulance availability and reducing delays across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer (i) AQW 35579/22-27; and (ii) AQW 36437/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 35579/22-27 on 11 December 2025 and AQW 36437/22-27 on 3 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her Department's new, permanent solution to the surface water flooding in the Marguerite area of Newcastle; and (ii) when it will be enacted.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and have been advised as follows:NI Water has undertaken additional targeted survey work and detailed analysis in the Marguerite Park area of the Newcastle catchment. This has included reviews of sewer network telemetry and rainfall data. These investigations, in tandem with NI Water’s supply chain consultants, have been important to ensure the hydraulic model accurately reflects current network performance such that a robust, long-term solution, which reduces the risk of out of sewer flooding, can be developed. NI Water anticipates that it will be able to share the findings of this work, together with any proposed solution(s) in Spring 2026. NI Water has implemented a temporary protocol in advance of the outcome of the modelling and the identification/delivery of any long-term solution. Any works identified as part of a solution will be subject to consideration in the next price control In addition, the gullies in this area are cleaned once a year and are on a priority list to be checked when the Department is notified of a weather warning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount she expects to raise, per annum, as a result of the proposed socialised grid connection charge.
answer_text: The decision on Fairer Grid Connection Costs remove unfairness and enable homes and businesses to connect to the grid at a fair price. There will be no direct revenue raised by my department by this measure. This decision will deliver fairness and balance regardless of your location across the region, it will help to encourage new investment in low carbon technologies and renewables, and it will place us on a more level playing field with our neighbours when it comes to attracting new business opportunities and renewable investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the average added annual cost of the proposed socialised grid connection charge to (i) domestic; and (ii) commercial, customers.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator has confirmed that the policy change to deliver fairer grid connection charges, does not constitute a reopening of NIE Networks’ RP7 price control determinisation and therefore there will be no additional NIE Networks’ charges for electricity consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the average reinforcement charge required under existing policy, to connect to the electricity grid where the local network is at capacity, for (i) residential properties; and (ii) for commercial premises.
answer_text: Prior to the decision on Fairer Grid Connection Costs, any consumer requesting a new connection to the electricity grid, where the local network is at capacity, is responsible for paying the full cost of any reinforcement works required. These costs vary significantly depending on location, network condition, and scale of the upgrade.		For domestic customers, reinforcement costs can range from a few thousand pounds for minor upgrades to tens of thousands in rural areas where the network is weaker. 	Under that system, domestic customers could face reinforcement costs that are prohibitively high, often exceeding £10,000 in cases requiring substantial line or transformer upgrades.		(ii) For commercial connections, reinforcement costs can vary more widely potentially, running from tens of thousands to several million pounds for large industrial sites requiring significant capacity increases.		These figures reflect the ‘first mover disadvantage’ inherent in the that system, where the first customer triggering reinforcement bore the full cost, allowing subsequent customers to benefit without contributing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any guidance issued to decision makers when assessing functional limitations in children with Autism Spectrum Disorder.
answer_text: My Department uses the Department for Work and Pensions’ Child Medical Guidance which provides guidance on the most common medical conditions relevant to Disability Living Allowance claims for children. This resource includes extensive guidance in relation to autism spectrum disorder and is available to read using the link below:https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/medical-guidance-for-dla-decision-makers-child-cases-staff-guide. Disability Living Allowance decision makers use the guidance when determining eligibility to ensure that the potential impact of autism spectrum disorder on an individual is recognised. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any formal protocols in place to control the level of the River Quoile, in anticipation of heavy rain over the winter.
answer_text: The water level in the Quoile River varies naturally with rainfall and tidal conditions. There are no formal protocols or infrastructure to actively control river levels in advance of heavy rainfall, given that the Quoile Barrier at Hare Island operates automatically via gravity-hinged flap gates that close under water pressure during high tides to prevent tidal inflow and reopen as tides fall to allow fluvial discharge. Whilst it is critical for preventing tidal flooding in Downpatrick and surrounding areas, the barrier is not designed to significantly reduce river levels proactively.To help reduce the impact of flooding during periods of adverse weather, Rivers operational staff undertake proactive maintenance of drainage infrastructure while actively monitoring river levels in real time through our network of hydrometric stations across the Downpatrick catchment. The Quoile Barrier is regularly checked to ensure maximum discharge during tidal windows, and temporary pumping is deployed as required. These combined efforts help keep designated watercourses and drainage infrastructure flowing freely and prevent flooding in many areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the recent surface flooding in the Market Place carpark, Downpatrick, and in the vicinity of Downpatrick and County Down Railway, to detail any interim emergency solutions in place to prevent more extensive flooding of the town centre, this winter.
answer_text: Aligned with the approach taken in Newcastle following the South East Flooding Review, the Department recognises the interaction between surface water drainage and high river levels in Downpatrick. Work is ongoing to establish a Downpatrick Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG) to ensure a coordinated approach to all flooding risks. Ahead of the next RCRG meeting it is proposed to convene drainage partners from across agencies to review the ongoing surface water flooding issues within Downpatrick Town Centre and consider whether any practical measures might be achievable.In preparation for this period of adverse weather, Rivers operational staff undertook proactive maintenance of drainage infrastructure and continued this work throughout the weekend, while actively monitoring river levels in real time, through our network of hydrometric stations, across the Downpatrick catchment. The Quoile barrier was operated to maximise discharge during tidal windows, and temporary pumping was deployed as required. These combined efforts helped keep designated watercourses and drainage infrastructure flowing freely and prevented flooding in many areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action her Department is taking to address the repeated flooding on the A2, between Newcastle and Annalong, during heavy rain.
answer_text: My Department has a responsibility to manage surface water which falls onto a road.  The owner or occupier of land adjoining a road is responsible for ensuring that surface water from their land does not flow onto or over the road. Similarly, landowners have a responsibility to ensure that drainage from a road is not impeded by a lack of maintenance of ‘drains’ within their property.The Department is actively investigating the drainage issues and will develop a programme of works to repair any affected systems.  Officials will liaise with all relevant landowners to ensure that their drainage systems do not allow water to flow onto the road or restrict the flow of water from the public road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a funding call was issued to all councils before allocating £135,000 towards festive lighting, in the Mid & East Antrim Borough Council area.
answer_text: It is for individual Councils, working in partnership with Development Offices, to identify their priorities for town centre regeneration funding and submit applications. Therefore, no funding call was made or was necessary as Councils can make applications at any point of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road gully maintenance machines her Department (i) owns; and (ii) contracts in.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) guidance; or (ii) regulations, in place in relation to minimum numbers and minimum dimensions for accessible parking bays on commercial sites, such as shopping centre car parks.
answer_text: Under legislation, my Department is responsible for the management and enforcement of on-street parking. There are a number of privately owned car parks, operating on private land, across the North, which are open to the public. The management and enforcement of these sites is a matter for the owner. The Department has no role in the design or provision of accessible parking bays in privately-owned car parks.The Department for Transport has, however, published the ‘Inclusive Mobility’ A Guide to Best Practice on Access to Pedestrian and Transport Infrastructure, which can be found here: https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/61d32bb7d3bf7f1f72b5ffd2/inclusive-mobility-a-guide-to-best-practice-on-access-to-pedestrian-and-transport-infrastructure.pdfSection 8 of this publication refers to accessible parking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any powers her Department has in relation to private estate management companies that charge annual fees for the maintenance of grassed and communal areas in residential developments.
answer_text: My Department does not have any specific powers in relation to private estate management companies. Such companies are not subject to sector-specific guidelines or statutory requirements, other than if they fall within general company law provisions.On foot of concerns raised by both individual residents and locally elected representatives in relation to the actions of property management companies and agents, my Department commissioned a scoping study to examine issues relating to the management of shared property. I have considered the report produced and have directed that a small team be set up to begin work on this in the next financial year, with a view to developing proposals for potential legislative reform. My Department will engage with stakeholders including home-owners and any bodies responsible for shared property management as part of that work.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to providing lightweight sandbag alternatives to residents of properties at risk of flooding, who cannot carry heavy sandbags.
answer_text: As the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to severe weather, my Department has well established plans to facilitate the response to flooding. This includes stores of sandbags at operational depots which are available for deployment by my staff, along with other resources, to assist when responding to flooding incidents.Through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG) there are also many publicly accessible sandbag stores located in communities where there is a history of flooding and where a local resilience group has been formed. As part of the ongoing work of the RCRG, communities are encouraged to develop their own community plan so that vulnerable members who may be unable to deploy sandbags are assisted by others from the local community, before the arrival of any responding government agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the new flood alleviation schemes in Newcastle were a contributing factor in the recent flooding in the Tullybrannigan area of Newcastle.
answer_text: The source of the recent flooding in the Tullybrannigan area of Newcastle remains under investigation, but initial findings suggest it may have been caused by extensive run-off from the Mourne Mountains after heavy overnight rain. I can advise that the previously completed flood alleviation schemes on the Shimna and Burren Rivers situated within another area of the town were not a contributing factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether she is aware of difficulties accessing the sandbag store at Islands Park, Newcastle, during recent flooding; and (ii) to detail the maintenance regime in place, to ensure that locks are in good working order.

answer_text: I have not had any engagement with the Minister of Finance in relation to amending building regulations to require water-saving features in new build housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, with reference to the ongoing Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs' consultation on its review of water efficiency standards in building regulations, to detail any consideration his Department has given to amending building regulations, to require water-saving features in new build housing.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department consulted on reviewing energy efficiency requirements and related areas of Building Regulations in 2023. Consideration was given in that consultation to the introduction of guidance that would provide designers with an option to demonstrate reduced water consumption (125 or fewer litres per person per day) for the purposes of compliance with Part F (Conservation of fuel and power) of the Building Regulations.   Introducing water efficiency guidance to Part F in this way would allow designers here to utilise the full benefits of the latest energy assessment software currently used in England, Wales and Scotland.  A paper seeking agreement to publish the response to that consultation is currently with the Executive. My officials will continue to keep a watching brief on developments elsewhere in this area for potential impacts on the energy efficiency of buildings.Water efficiency policy and legislation is the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure and the introduction of regulations specifically for water efficiency would be for other departments to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will publish the report on her Department's consultation on Developer Contributions for Wastewater Infrastructure.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure. The consultation ran for 14 weeks and sought views on options for a possible pathway to introduce developer contributions – either on a voluntary basis or in the form of a compulsory levy – which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock the capacity of our sewerage network.293 responses to the consultation were received.  These responses are currently being considered with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme will continue to approve all applications that meet the current eligibility criteria throughout the remainder of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all instances, within the last five years, where storm-related damage has led to the closure of (i) a road; (ii) a section of active travel infrastructure; and (ii) the suspension of a public transport service, including the duration of each closure or suspension.
answer_text: During any significant weather event, the priority of my department is to ensure the safety of both my staff and the public.Every event causes damage to parts of the road network within the North on both main arterial routes and smaller minor and rural roads.  Due to the volume of reports during such events, my department prioritises informing the public of significant road closures via Traffic Watch NI, but does not hold records of each individual closure or the duration of such a closure.Due to the widespread nature of rail and bus services across the region, the impact of storms will vary depending on the location. The most recent Storm Amy led to severe disruption across the network on 3rd October 2025 from 5pm onwards with bus services in the North West cancelled from 6pm onwards. All services were fully restored the next day. Rail services were also impacted with a suspension of all services from 5pm on 3rd October, and partial suspensions on 4th.Storms Eowyn, Darragh, Bert and Ciaran all led to severe and widespread disruption to the bus and rail network.As a result of a power outage caused by Storm Eowyn, the Strangford Lough Ferry service was unable to operation from 5pm on 24 January until 9am on 25th January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the 2019 guidance on Supporting Transgender Young People, applied in youth service settings.
answer_text: The 2019 guidance applied to pre-schools, primary schools, post-primary and special schools, as well as other educational settings under the control of the Education Authority, including the EA Youth Service and EOTAS centres.This guidance has been withdrawn as of 8th September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following her Department's launch of the electric vehicle cross pavement charging trial, to detail the engagement she has had with the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles, in relation to altering the eligibility criteria for the Electric Vehicle Chargepoint Grant for those who rent or own a flat, to include homes without access to off-street parking.
answer_text: Officials from my Department attend quarterly and monthly meetings with the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). These meetings are an opportunity to update each other on developments, including the development of our cross pavement charging solutions, and seek clarification on issues such as eligibility on the various grants made available by OZEV.Further information, including eligibility criteria, on the grants available from OZEV can be found at the following link: Electric vehicle chargepoint grants - GOV.UK. The ‘Electric Vehicle Chargepoint Grant for Households with On-Street Parking’ is not restricted to house owners and includes options for those who own or rent a flat.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following her Department's launch of the electric vehicle cross pavement charging trial, to detail the engagement she has had with the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles, in relation to introducing Electric Vehicle Chargepoint Grants for house owners, without access to off-street parking.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the drafting of a new Racial Equality Strategy for 2026 and beyond.
answer_text: Work is actively underway to develop a new Framework for Race Relations beyond 2025, building on the foundations laid by the current Racial Equality Strategy.Development of the draft Framework has been informed by engagement with a range of stakeholders from the Minority Ethnic sector to ensure it reflects lived experiences. Engagement is also ongoing with wider society, through a research project. We want to ensure the views and experiences of all in our communities shape the key priorities and interventions in order to achieve the draft Framework’s vision and outcomes. We hope to consult on the Framework in the coming months when we will welcome feedback on identifying preventative and effective interventions to tackle inequalities and create a more prosperous society for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, with reference to her Department's consultation on introducing fees for some statutory planning functions, to detail the consideration given to the average cost to local councils of processing applications for (i) non-material changes; and (ii) discharge of conditions, when setting the proposed fee of £115.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the cost of processing such applications. The Consultation proposals aim to promote best practice and improve the overall efficiency and quality of the planning process, in the public interest of recovering costs in the planning system. Further analysis of the proposed fees will be undertaken following the conclusion of the consultation period. I would encourage anyone with an interest in the planning process to respond to the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) average number of applications for (a) non-material changes; and (b) discharge of conditions, that are made to local councils each year.
answer_text: Figures from the Northern Ireland Planning Statistics from the ten-year period between the 2015/16 published statistics until 2024/25, published 26th June 2025, show that there has been an annual average of 853 discharge of condition applications and 467 applications for non-material change received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to local councils for processing applications for (i) non-material changes; and (ii) discharge of conditions.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the cost of processing such applications, you may wish to contact councils to request this information.
