question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 21599/22-27, to detail the (i) criteria; and (ii) threshold, required to trigger a departmental intervention under Article 4 of the Harbours (Northern Ireland) Order 2002.
answer_text: My Department has no specific criteria or threshold in respect of triggering Article 4 of the Harbours (Northern Ireland) Order 2002. Any consideration of intervention under this power would be assessed on a case by case basis. To date, my Department has not exercised this power.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department took with respect to implementing Article 3(1) of The Activity Centres (Young Persons' Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, while this matter fell under the remit of the Department of Education.
answer_text: The Activity Centres (Young Persons' Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 came into operation in June 1998. Subordinate legislation was not made under the 1998 Order by my department before the transfer of responsibility for The Activity Centres (Young Person’s Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 on  1 December 1999 to the Department of Culture Arts and Leisure.Responsibility moved to the Department for Communities in 2016.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he, and his predecessors, have had with the Department for Education in relation to implementing Article 3(1) of the Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, since the Order was made.
answer_text: The Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 transferred from the Department of Education to the Department for Culture, Arts and Leisure (DCAL) under the Transfer and Assignment Functions Order (NI) 1999 following devolution and subsequently to the Department for Communities following the restructuring of Departments in 2016.There has been no engagement with the Department for Education in relation to the implementation of this Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department has taken with respect to implementing Article 3(1) of the Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, since this matter has fallen under the remit of the Department for Communities.
answer_text: To date no action has been taken by the Department for Communities with respect to implementing Article 3(1) of the Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.The Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 transferred from the Department of Education to the Department for Culture, Arts and Leisure (DCAL) under the Transfer and Assignment Functions Order (NI) 1999 following devolution and subsequently to the Department for Communities following the restructuring of departments in 2016.In 2007, DCAL through Sport NI, commissioned Adventure Activities Associates Ltd to complete research to consider implementation of statutory licensing requirements for Adventure Activity providers in Northern Ireland. The report found that a voluntary rather than statutory scheme would best suit Northern Ireland and was the preferred option by the industry and other representatives.  Adventuremark was adopted as the recommended voluntary accreditation scheme in Northern Ireland. The Department and Sport NI encourage all Adventure Activity Providers to complete this accreditation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans, including a timeline, with respect to implementing Article 3(1) of The Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
answer_text: At present there are no plans by my Department with respect to implementing Article 3(1) of The Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.A Voluntary Accreditation Scheme is in place in Northern Ireland for Adventure Activity Providers, Advernturemark is the recommended accreditation by my Department and Sport NI.Officials from my Department are engaging with Sport NI and other relevant stakeholders to review the current approach and consider what arrangements may be most appropriate for Northern Ireland going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide his assessment of how the safety of young people, in a paid-for adventure experience, is being ensured (i) in the absence of the licensing scheme that is required under The Activity Centres (Young Persons’ Safety) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998; and (ii) in light of the closure of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre, for the training and accreditation of adventure sport instructors to nationally recognised health and safety standards.
answer_text: At present in Northern Ireland there is no statutory licencing requirement for Adventure Activity Providers however a Voluntary Accreditation Scheme operates which providers are encouraged to complete. Adventuremark is the recommended accreditation in Northern Ireland by my Department and Sport NI.National Governing Bodies (NGB) that operate in the outdoor sport environment deliver accreditation to their members and instructors who can then go on to use training centres, such as Tollymore National Outdoor Centre or the natural environment. During the temporary closure of Tollymore National Outdoor Centre individuals can participate in training/accreditation through a number of other training centres throughout Northern Ireland that provide the recognised NGB training/accreditation for Adventure Sports Instructors, these are listed below:Belfast Activity Centre (BAC)Crawfordsburn Activity CentreGreenhill YMCA Outdoor Education CentreLorne EstateBryson Energy – Pathway Adventure ActivitiesTodds Leap

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQO 2391/22-27,  to outline (i) whether her Department has always been open to considering alternative Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD) proposals outside of the tourism pillar or whether this is a recent change; (ii) whether she is aware of any potential innovation projects that might be brought forward; and (iii) whether her Department will, in principle, be open to exploring alternative proposals under any of the four pillars of investment covered by the BRCD.
answer_text: My focus is on supporting Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and the wider Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD) partners to develop the right project, irrespective of pillar, that will retain the funding in the region and deliver sustained and inclusive economic growth. My officials continue to engage with the Council to help deliver that outcome.The reallocation of funding, including any decision to fund a project within another pillar, will, in the first instance, be considered and agreed by the BRCD partners.  I am aware that it is the partners’ initial preference that a project within the Tourism and Regeneration pillar of the Deal be considered, given its importance to delivering inclusive sub-regional growth and addressing economic inactivity in both our urban and rural communities.Any proposals submitted by the BRCD partners will be considered by the Department that has policy responsibility for the outcomes that the project is delivering. My Department’s policy responsibilities on City Deals are around Tourism, Innovation and Digital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to reduce the waiting list requirement under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to less than a year.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme was initiated on a phased approach. The first phase granted reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (Rol). Phase Two, launched 15 September 2025, extends access to the wider European Union.I have also decided to reduce the qualifying period from two years to one year, and by widening access to the EU, I am giving more patients more options to get the treatment they need.This scheme is about listening to patients, being flexible, and taking practical action to ease pressure on waiting lists. This decision is a data informed decision supplemented by active listening across the service and delivering on my commitment to best maximise this important initiative for patient benefit and waiting list reduction. At this time, we have no further plan to adjust the Scheme parameters and will continue to monitor its uptake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action she is taking to improve the affordability of childcare for those in further and higher education.
answer_text: The Executive is committed to high quality, affordable childcare as a key enabler for an inclusive, thriving economy.	There are practical supports in place in both Further and Higher Education to help with childcare costs, , as well as in our range of skills initiatives.		In Further Education, Further Education Grants, the Further Education Hardship Fund and the Care to Learn scheme aim to address barriers to education including costs of childcare to enable individuals to fulfil their potential. 		My Department is conducting a review of all further education student support measures including childcare, to ensure funding is targeted fairly, tackles the most significant barriers and delivers value for money. 	The Higher Education Student Support Regulations include two specific childcare support schemes.The first is the Childcare Grant which offers help to meet childcare costs for students with dependent children aged under 15 (or 17 if they have special educational needs) in registered or approved childcare. The second is the Parents’ Learning Allowance which offers help with course-related costs for students with dependent children. I am committed to continue working with the Minister of Education on the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the processing of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's new Road Maintenance Strategy.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of road network and vital role it plays in connecting our people and communities as well as supporting the wider economy.For many years my Department has operated with a constrained maintenance budget due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government, and this has resulted in an under-investment in our infrastructure and a deterioration in the condition of our roads.That is why I have asked my officials to develop a new approach to road maintenance. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene. It will be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner.My officials are finalising work on a draft road maintenance strategy which I will then share in the coming weeks.Public finances are limited, and we have a lot to do to make up for years of austerity and underfunding but I am committed to ensuring that people are at the heart of funding decisions, and that we do all that we can to be efficient and effective in the delivery of public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how the 2025-26 Budget reflects the Programme for Government 2024-2027 priority of green growth.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans for investment in wastewater infrastructure in the Rathfriland area, with a view to resolving the development bans currently in place.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however my officials have sought the relevant information. NI Water has advised that the Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) serving Rathfriland is currently operating very close to its design capacity and NI Water is experiencing compliance issues with the Water Order Consent in relation to the discharge to the public sewer. The consequence of this is that there are development constraints within this catchment, and NI Water is currently recommending that connections cannot be approved, as sufficient wastewater treatment capacity is not available at present for any proposed developments.NI Water has no plans to upgrade Rathfriland WwTW at this point in time, however the treatment works will be included in the business plan for the next regulatory Price Control period, PC28. Any proposed upgrade would be subject to the outcome of the Price Control process in consultation with the regulators, and subject to the availability of sufficient public funding.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26109/22-27, to detail how the £636,045 of Direct Payment funding that was returned unspent to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in the 2024-25 financial year, was subsequently spent.
answer_text: The Direct Payment funding that was returned to SEHSCT in 2024/25 was used to support the delivery of Trust services in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on odour issues in the Warrenpoint area.
answer_text: I am aware that during the weekend of the 28-29 June 2025 my department received several reports regarding odours emanating from Warrenpoint Harbour. The source of this odour was linked to other Warrenpoint Harbour activities that my department has no regulatory remit over.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what strategies he is exploring to augment youth work practice, in light of recent civil unrest.
answer_text: My officials attended a roundtable discussion on 7 July with the First Minister and deputy First Minister to discuss the recent civil unrest.(ii)  In response to recent civil unrest The Education Authority (EA) activated its Major Emergency Plan arrangements for education settings and youth provision. Throughout the period of unrest, the EA has engaged with schools in the Ballymena area and wider school community and has fully participated in the multi-agency coordination structures led by the PSNI to represent schools and youth provision.The EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) is providing advice, guidance and support to schools both around child and family support issues and teaching, learning and curriculum resources.  Social Workers and Teachers from IES are helping schools engage with families and children who have been displaced or impacted by the unrest. The IES and other EA services are working with schools to plan for a return-to-work support plan from late August onwards to help schools deal with any additional wellbeing needs and reintegration support requests.TEO has advised that relevant agencies worked collectively and at pace in response and officials will maintain the positive coordination and engagement established as we move from response to recovery. TEO has taken responsibility for chairing the Community Confidence Group (CCG) which was set up by the PSNI in response to the recent unrest. The role of this group will be to examine how all agencies and partners work together to enable recovery in the areas affected by the violence. In the longer term, focus will be on how racism and hate can be tackled as part of TEO’s wider good relations policies. This includes in the development of the new approach to Racial Equality and in the implementation of the Refugee Integration Strategy. To inform development of the strategic approach, officials are working to develop engagement sessions with the Education Authority, aimed at children and young people, that focus on racial equality and early intervention strategies The Planned Interventions Programme continues to fund crucial activity to engage those young people most at risk of entering the youth justice system and/or paramilitary recruitment. Through the provision of positive alternatives for our young people, it seeks to mitigate the impacts anti-social behaviour can have on community tensions and in turn good relations. In 2024/25, TEO allocated £400k to the delivery of PIP for 2024/25. DE allocated an additional £300k funding to PIP (for 2024/25 only) to enable funding of a total of 122 groups. Education continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands, associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations, which the current 2025-26 budget allocation will not fully address.  Education currently faces an overspend of c£348m for 2025-26 for inescapable pressures and pre-committed pressures alone.The Department of Finance has advised departments t

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether the Housing Executive is aware of flooding at Dunwellan Park, Newcastle, in Autumn 2023; and (ii) to provide copies of any records the Housing Executive may hold in relation to this incident.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive local office are aware of flooding which affected a small number of Housing Executive properties in Dunwellan Park from 31/10/2023 - 1/11/2023.These properties appear on the Housing Executive’s flood risk register.  The Housing Executive through our response maintenance contractor provided sandbags for a number of properties and subsequent repairs (dehumidifiers / boiler checks for flooded boiler etc).In relation to point (ii) of this question, this is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and the member should submit a request directly with the Housing Executive asking them to provide any records they may hold in relation to this incident.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Narrow Water Bridge project, whether he has given any consideration to the development of Narrow Water Castle.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why her Department's loans to NI Water were not structured to include principal debt repayment.
answer_text: The department’s loan agreements with NI Water were developed under The Water and Sewerage Services (NI) Order 2006, which enables my department to provide loans for the purpose of financing NI Water’s capital investment programme. The loan instrument is structured to provide a return to the department from the interest accruing on borrowings issued. While the return does not include an ongoing repayment of the principal debt, the 2006 Order provides the department with powers to address the repayment of the loan, either by write off the principal and interest of any loans, or to enter into an arrangement for the conversion into share capital of the principal and interest of loans so made. My officials are currently working with NI Water to progress the options for addressing the first loan agreement, the redemption of which is due March 2027. These options include the refinancing of the existing debt for a further period, or for the conversion of debt to share equity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether surface water outfalls in the Dunwellan area, Newcastle, will be included in any permanent solution to the surface water flooding in Newcastle.
answer_text: I have liaised with NI Water and they have advised as follows:(i) 	NI Water carried out investigations of the storm sewer network in the Dunwellan area of Newcastle following Storm Ciarán in 2023. At that time, all three surface water outlets were found to be functioning as designed.(ii) 	given the time that has passed since those works, the network was re-examined in 2025. During this most recent assessment, it was determined that a significant build-up of silt has reoccurred, preventing full completion of the investigation and as a result, a 28-day desilting programme has been scheduled with NI Water’s contractor. Once complete, investigations will resume to ensure the network continues to operate effectively.(iii) 	NI Water remains committed to maintaining and improving the resilience of storm water infrastructure in this area.

Once complete, investigations will resume to ensure the network continues to operate effectively.(iii) 	NI Water remains committed to maintaining and improving the resilience of storm water infrastructure in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the options she is considering in relation to dealing with the NI Water loan note debt that matures in March 2027; and (ii) the engagement she has had with the Minister of Finance in this regard.
answer_text: (i) NI Water is considering two options for the 2027 Loan Note in terms of refinancing the existing debt with new borrowing or a debt-for-equity swap in which the loan value is exchanged for share capital.(ii) My department has been consulting with officials in the Department of Finance to assist with the analysis of the options in terms of assessing the impact of each option on public finances and NI Water customers, and I will engage with the Minister of Finance once this work has determined that the Minister’s approval is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the reasons why approval for the business case for repairs to the modular building at St Francis Primary School, Drumaroad, is still outstanding; and (ii) whether the process can be fast-tracked.
answer_text: The business case for repairs to the modular building at St Francis’ Primary School, Drumaroad, was received from the Education Authority (EA) on 21 May 2025. My officials have been reviewing the business case, alongside other urgent priorities, to ensure that the business case tests the feasible options and to confirm that the preferred option represents value for money. The business case is now at the final stage of approval. The school will continue to be kept informed as the scheme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW28109/22-27, for her assessment of the reasons why a flap valve was not originally fitted at the outfall in Eliza Close, if it was a vitally important aspect of the system.
answer_text: I have liaised with NI Water and they have advised as follows:(i)	the absence of a flap valve at the Eliza Close outfall was identified during investigative works following Storm Ciarán in late 2023. This issue came to light as part of NI Water’s post-event response and subsequent drainage infrastructure review to identify contributing factors to localised flooding. (ii)	NI Water has completed the necessary design work for the installation of the flap valve. A contractor has been appointed, and works are scheduled for completion by the end of July 2025, weather and site conditions permitting.(iii)	the original design of the outfall at Eliza Close did not incorporate a flap valve. At the time of installation, approximately 15 years ago, the infrastructure met regulatory standards and local drainage conditions. However, changing climate patterns and more frequent high-intensity storm events have since highlighted the need for enhanced protections such as non-return valves. The current installation reflects NI Water’s commitment to improving resilience in flood-prone areas in response to evolving environmental pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 28107/22-27, (i) to provide further detail on the options explored in NI Water's modelling work in relation to reducing the risk of out-of-sewer flooding in the Marguerite area of Newcastle; and (ii) whether NI Water will engage with the Newcastle Regional Community Resilience Group prior to taking a decision.
answer_text: I have liaised with NI Water and they have advised as follows:(i) 	Whilst a possible storage solution has been identified, further refinement is required through modelling and additional surveys, to ensure the possibility of localised out of sewer flooding is reduced as far as possible. When considering solutions to reduce the risk of out of sewer flooding, NI Water will run various scenarios through their model and may carryout additional site surveys to ensure assumptions made are correct. NI Water will always endeavour to find the best solution in economic, engineering buildability and environmental terms.(ii) 	As above, any solution identified will be the best solution in economic, engineering buildability and environmental terms, taking into account the layout of our existing infrastructure at the location. Once NI Water is satisfied that the solution referred to above provides the optimum protection possible to reduce this risk of out of sewer flooding from our network, the details of the solution will be shared with the Newcastle Regional Community Resilience Group. Any works proposed following the modelling works will be subject to budget availability.

answer_text: I have liaised with NI Water and they have advised as follows:(i)	the absence of a flap valve at the Eliza Close outfall was identified during investigative works following Storm Ciarán in late 2023. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider increasing the cap or reducing the homeowner contribution for works carried out under the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme.
answer_text: My Department’s Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme is aimed specifically at residential properties that have flooded internally and remain vulnerable to frequent flooding. The original financial thresholds of the grant scheme covered 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs and had not changed since the scheme was launched in 2016. The homeowner is also required to contribute 10% of the survey and estimated installation costs and any additional cost that may be incurred above £10,000. I had recently requested my officials to carry out a review of this financial threshold to take account of any inflationary increases since 2016 to the present day using the Consumer Price Index (CPIH) rate, which includes owner occupied housing costs. I am pleased to advise that I have recently approved the increase of the financial threshold for the scheme from the original £10,000 to a new £13,700 threshold. This came into effect from 01 April 2025, and it is proposed that similar inflationary uplifts are considered across future financial years. The homeowner will still be required to contribute 10% of the survey and estimated installation costs and any additional cost that may be incurred above the £13,700 threshold.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the most effective approaches for reducing adult reoffending.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 - AQO 2225/22-27			   ANSWER  Reducing adult reoffending is a complex area and one that requires a joined up, whole of government approach to address the root causes of offending behaviour. That is why I welcome the Executive’s Programme for Government commitment to develop a cross-Executive strategic approach.  A number of factors tend to be prevalent for many who engage in offending behaviour – including poor mental health, addictions, lack of suitable accommodation, poverty, along with a lack of educational attainment and employment opportunities. Research consistently shows that rehabilitation is pivotal, both in custody and the community, to address these complex needs.  Evidence shows that community sentences can have better outcomes, compared with short custodial sentences, which can often compound an individual’s problems.  They may well lose their accommodation, employment, and their family connection or support network. If not lost, connections are certainly weakened.  We also know that intervening early has the ability to break the cycle of offending, while trauma and adverse childhood experiences often underlie offending behaviour.  That is why we are working closely with colleagues across government to develop the new cross-Executive strategy.  Adopting a more person-centred, preventative approach and implementing initiatives to tackle wider societal issues that contribute to offending behaviour will be important.    Reducing adult reoffending not only improves outcomes for individuals but also helps build safer communities. By working together, we can support people to move away from offending.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Knockevin School, Dundrum, will be included in the third tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits outside schools.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools.  The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.  I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in this financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the this financial year and I intend to make an announcement on the next tranche in the coming weeks.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on plans for support for the fishing industry.
answer_text: I expect to make an announcement on the opening of the Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund (MEFF) by the end of this month after I have reviewed my officials’ proposals for this financial year.The MEFF will provide £2.75m in financial assistance to support investment in progressing a range of themes that industry has indicated are priorities. These include workforce recruitment initiatives, automation of on-board practices, economic and environmental resilience of the fleet and, a continuing focus on the de-carbonising fishing activities.In addition, I plan to write to Minister Daniel Zeichner to stress the need for Northern Ireland to secure an equitable share of the recently announced £360m Fisheries and Coastal Growth Fund. I will also be seeking an assurance that my officials will be fully and meaningfully involved in all aspects of the Fund’s design, delivery and monitoring to ensure the specific needs and priorities of our stakeholders are to the fore and that it the benefits from the available support can be maximised.My officials have also commenced a review the Harbour Development Plan within the original Fishing and Seafood Development Programme to determine which elements within the original proposals remain appropriate and, subject to affordability, could be progressed over the next few years. My officials are engaging with the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) to determine the feasibility for my department to directly engage with Ireland regarding access to the 6-12 nautical mile zone rather than via Defra.  Officials are also due to meet Isle of Man officials later this month concerning range of issues raised to date.In terms of labour, I continue to make the case to the UK Government for changes in migration policy reflecting the concerns and proposed interventions articulated by the fishing industry. Work to explore possibilities for apprenticeships continues alongside ways to increase marketing opportunities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the implementation of a permanent solution to the pumping station in Marguerite Avenue, Newcastle; and (ii) to detail the anticipated timescale.
answer_text: I have been advised by NI Water that there are no plans to upgrade the Burren River Tullymore Wastewater Pumping station located at Marguerite Park, Newcastle.  However, to help reduce the risk of flooding during extreme weather events, NI Water has installed a level monitor and a protocol is now in place to ensure NI Water’s contractor will be dispatched with a pump to use until the levels in the river subside.  Since this protocol was implemented, the pump has been deployed to site on a number of occasions and worked effectively. NI Water is undertaking modelling work to establish if there are any changes that could be made to the layout of its infrastructure in the area to assist in further reducing the risk of out of sewer flooding during extreme weather events. The modelling is anticipated to complete within the next month, however the delivery of any associated capital works for any solution identified will be subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any existing proposals to reduce the risk of flooding in (i) Eliza Close; and (ii) Dunwellan Park, Newcastle.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with multi-agency partners, including Local Government, to support local communities affected by flooding through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG). A resilience group has been established in Newcastle following flooding in 2020 which has resulted in some positive outcomes in relation to additional resources provided, information sharing and enhanced communication channels.(i) I have been advised by NI Water that work was conducted at Eliza Close following Storm Ciaran to alleviate flooding by installing an outfall.  It was however subsequently discovered that a flap valve had not been fitted at the outfall.  NI Water has now arranged for a contractor to install the required flap valve. (ii)NIW has advised that there are no indications of flooding at Dunwellan Park during previous storms. The cause of recent minor flooding at this site was due to misuse of the system in this area, particularly due to wet wipes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with the UK Government in relation to protecting properties built after 2009 from flooding, including any response received.
answer_text: My Department engaged with the UK Government when the Flood Re scheme was being established in 2014 to ensure that the North was included. Flood Re’s 2009 criterion was set by the UK Government and reflected that, by 1st January 2009, planning policy and flood mapping was in place to discourage residential development in flood plains. There is ongoing engagement between my Department and DEFRA on the Flood Re scheme as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans for upgrading the drainage and sewage system in Bryansford Avenue, Newcastle, and the surrounding flood area.
answer_text: The Department currently has no planned drainage works in Bryansford Avenue, Newcastle. However, Rivers Directorate will continue its routine inspection and maintenance of designated watercourses and associated infrastructure within the Newcastle catchment.I have been advised that NI Water has no reports of flooding at this location, apart from a routine blockage which was reported in July 2022 caused by wet wipes.  As a consequence, NI Water has no plans to upgrade the infrastructure in this specific location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any Departmental or Ministerial decisions taken in relation to the Mourne Gateway Project; and (ii) when these decisions were taken.
answer_text: Minister Lyons approved Newry Mourne and Down District Council’s Outline Business Case (OBC) for the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project on 19 November 2021.Due to changes that occurred over the passage of time, an update to the OBC was prepared by Council and approved by the Department’s Permanent Secretary on 19 March 2024, following which the Department awarded a contract for funding for the project in May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the new road maintenance strategy will prioritise those local council areas which have the highest number of road defects.
answer_text: The new approach to how the road network is maintained will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene. It will be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner. In distributing the resources available for road repairs and resurfacing programmes, allocations relating to the areas covered by the Department’s four Roads Divisions will continue to be made on the basis of need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity. The Divisions also use a range of indicators when apportioning budget across District Council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds according to need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she is having with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to develop an alternative to the Mourne Gateway Project.
answer_text: I am supportive of retaining the funding that was committed to the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project within in the Newry, Mourne and Down area, and I am committed to working with partners to help achieve that.My officials, including Tourism NI, have met with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and Belfast Region City Deal representatives to offer support with the development of alternative options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the new road maintenance strategy.
answer_text: My officials have been developing a new approach to road maintenance. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene. It will be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner. While work continues on bringing forward specific actions, the delivery of this new approach will be dependent on future budget settlements which have yet to be finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount her Department has spent on costs relating to the Mourne Gateway Project.
answer_text: My Department utilised £0.5 million of Belfast Region City Deal funding to grant fund project costs to support Newry Mourne and Down District Council to explore the viability of the project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any actions that have been taken to improve preparations for civil contingencies during the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: We have established and well-practiced civil contingencies structures in place here, and we are continuously striving to improve how we prepare for, respond to, and recover from emergencies. Our Civil Contingencies Framework procedures were last used in January with Storm Éowyn, and we are taking action to implement lessons identified from that event. A review of the framework is planned for later this year. Within the 2025/26 financial year a number of steps will be taken in relation to our ongoing preparations for future events. These include the continuing roll out of the learning and development strategy, further development of the Risk Register and our involvement in a major pandemic preparedness and response exercise later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be targeted plans for patients that have waited on a treatment waiting list for two years or more but cannot afford the upfront costs of the Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme is one of several initiatives being funded to start tackling the backlog of patients awaiting assessment and treatment. Tackling the waiting list backlog will initially focus on patients waiting over four years for assessment or treatment. Building the workforce and infrastructure required to sustainably reduce the length of time patients are waiting will take time. Independent sector support will be required to achieve this through both insourcing and outsourcing across a range of specialties including Ophthalmology, Orthopaedics, General Surgery, Gynaecology and ENT. Investment will be made to build core capacity, particularly in areas where the infrastructure is already in place. Endoscopy and diagnostic imaging will be of initial focus, with further investment planned in Urology, Breast Surgery, Dermatology and Cardiac Surgery. Trauma capacity will also be expanded to improve access for fracture patients, which will also benefit orthopaedic capacity.Other initiatives include:Targeting funding at specialist procedures, particularly for children and women. Setting up Mega clinics to provide patients with a ‘one stop shop’ where they can get surgical review and anaesthetic preoperative assessment in a single appointment.Expansion of Primary Care Elective Service capacity in dermatology, minor surgery and gynaecology.Development of partnership working with the voluntary sector to deliver a Waiting Well Programme and a Cancer Charities Programme. Taken collectively, these measures will improve access to elective care for all patients.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two. Investment at this level or higher will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will consider introducing a new funding scheme with a higher cap to facilitate significant repairs that could not be facilitated through funding under the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme.
answer_text: When it became clear that the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme would cost less than anticipated, the option to increase the £100k cap was considered.  However, advice from officials was that this would be difficult to justify on Value-for-Money grounds as the majority of impacted businesses had re-opened.  The majority of businesses receiving the enhanced grant also received the £7,500 provided under the Flood Damage Business Grant Scheme as well as rate relief. This level of support was significantly more generous than the flood support offered in Britain. The maximum grant provided via the EFSS also exceeded the €100,000 offered by the South in its Enhanced Emergency Business Flood Relief Scheme - that scheme was also more restrictive in comparison to the EFSS in that it was only available to businesses with up to 20 employees whereas the EFSS was open to businesses with up to 250 employees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has any plans to provide further funding towards revitalising Downpatrick town centre in the 2025-26 financial year, given that less than half of the support funding allocated following the 2023 autumn floods in the South East was spent.
answer_text: Whilst Conor Murphy agreed to allocate up to £10 million from his 2024/25 budget to deliver three schemes, the cost of delivering the schemes was lower than anticipated.  When it became clear that this was the case, Minister Murphy asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments for flood related spend, but given the time frame available, no realistic options were identified.  Any unspent money was reallocated across the Department of Economy to meet other capital pressures. My department is leading on setting up a cross-departmental group to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding and this is something that Downpatrick businesses will potentially benefit from.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she is aware of any local businesses that decided not to apply for funding under the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme due to the cost of repairs significantly exceeding the £100,000 cap.
answer_text: I am not aware of any businesses who decided not to apply to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme due to the cost of repairs significantly exceeding the £100,000 cap.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and local businesses to understand the reasons for the significant underspend on flood support funding allocated to support local businesses in Downpatrick and Newry affected by floods in 2023.
answer_text: My department has had on-going engagement with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council officials over the duration of the scheme.   My understanding is that the total cost of the schemes is lower than estimated due to 1. lower than anticipated application numbers and 2. The total eligible costs incurred by applicants to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme (EFSS) was lower than anticipated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any options her Department and Tourism NI are exploring following the landowner's withdrawal from negotiations with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project.
answer_text: In the first instance, examining alternative options would be a decision for the Belfast Region City Deal Executive Board.  The Board has not presented my Department nor Tourism Northern Ireland with any alternative proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her predecessor had with local businesses to assess the level of need prior to setting the £100,000 cap on applications for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme.
answer_text: Conor Murphy engaged with a number of businesses and business organisations following the severe flooding at the end of 2023.  Departmental officials made use of a range of material when assessing the need for the flood support schemes.  This material included a survey of a sample of flood affected businesses provided by Newry Mourne and Down District Council, a report undertaken by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre (UUEPC) on the economic impact of the flooding and information proved by the NI Federation of Small Businesses (FSB).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment works in Irish Street, Downpatrick.
answer_text: My Department is currently preparing a business case to assess and define how best to secure a preferred developer for the Irish St Development Site.  Subject to a positive business case, and funding being available, a development brief for the site will launch later in 2025. My Department is working closely with Newry Mourne and Down District Council and the Downpatrick Regeneration Working Group to take forward the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department decided on a cap of £100,000 for applications to the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme.
answer_text: The level of support offered by the EFSS was the most generous on these islands. In England, Scotland and Wales the equivalent support offered to flooded businesses was between £2,500 and £5,000. The £100,000 cap is similar to the Irish Government's Enhanced Emergency Business Flood Relief Scheme which offered support of up to €100,000 for eligible businesses. However, that scheme was more restrictive in comparison to the EFSS in that it was only available to businesses with up to 20 employees whereas the EFSS was open to businesses with less than 250 employees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQT 1235/22-27, and having seen his officials' options paper, whether he is content to proceed with reinstating the cross-border scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025 and will give patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.  Patients will only be able to access the Scheme once they have waited on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for two years.The new Scheme will be initiated on a phased approach.  The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (RoI). The second phase will be introduced at a later date and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European Union (EU).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25600/22-27, to detail the (i) amount; and (ii) proportion of Direct Payment funding that was returned unspent to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: (i) The amount of Direct Payment funding that was returned unspent to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in the 2024-25 financial year was £636,045.(ii) The proportion of Direct Payment funding that was returned unspent to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in the 2024-25 financial year was 3.58%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25600/22-27, how his Department (i) assesses the effectiveness of Direct Payments; and (ii) ensures that returned Direct Payments are appropriately accounted for if it is unable to identify the amount that is returned unspent.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has for a relief road or bypass to address congestion in Downpatrick town centre.
answer_text: I am aware of longstanding issues with traffic congestion in Downpatrick, particularly at the convergence of Irish Street, Church Street and Market Street in the historic town centre. A feasibility study was published by my Department in 2018 which examined the case for constructing an Eastern Distributor Road in Downpatrick. The alignment of the route was taken from the Ards Area Plan and considered a new connection between the Strangford Road towards the Saul Road and the Ardglass Road. This detailed feasibility study (Downpatrick Eastern Distributor Road Feasibility Report | Department for Infrastructure) concluded that the proposed road and its alignment offered limited relief for Downpatrick Town Centre and its main benefits would be accessing developer land zonings DK-07, DK-08, DK-12, and DK-13. Plans to build around 1,100 new homes on a portion of these zoned lands were unveiled by a private developer in July 2024, which included a developer-led 1.5km link road between the Saul Road and Ballyhornan Road to facilitate access and accommodate additional road users. This route broadly follows a section of the proposed alignment considered as part of the Eastern Distributor Road feasibility study.The final decision for granting permissions on such a scheme remains a matter for Newry, Mourne and Down District Council’s Planning Committee, and therefore my Department has no current plans to build a relief road or bypass.

answer_text: In October 2024, I announced £13.1 million of recurrent funding with up to £2m funding available in year (2024/25), to improve services for Children with Disabilities. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25484/22-27, how the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust assesses need or places children with complex needs in residential care if it does not keep a record of families that have requested it.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust holds this information on personal files in relation to all parental requests for residential care, however this information is not centralised on an accessible information management system.  Locating this information would require a manual trawl of hundreds of files. During February 2024, the DoH introduced a new information gathering template to collate information on the numbers of children in Residential Care, those availing of Residential Short Breaks and those on the Edge of Care.  This analysis alongside the 6-monthly submission of the Statutory Functions Report and Corporate Parenting Reports to the DoH SPPG, provides information for both the Trust and DoH SPPG to assess the need for children with complex needs for Residential Care. The South Eastern HSC Trust is engaged with the Strategic Reform Board, and the associated Children’s Social Care Workstreams. There is also work ongoing within the Residential Care Workstream in respect of the Projected Residential Care Need for Children with Disability across all five HSC Trusts. This work (alongside other work being completed across the Children’s Social Care Reform Workstreams) will assist with informing the future commissioning of services, in accordance with the Departmental Children’s Disability Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service operations in response to landscape fires in each of the last ten years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to build a shared, cross-departmental outcomes framework to track collective impact on suicide prevention, not just within his own Department.
answer_text: An updated Action Plan and Implementation Plan has been developed for Protect Life 2. These plans were discussed and agreed at the Executive Working Group on 3 April subject to funding. Once published the Department and the Public Health Agency will be developing enhanced monitoring arrangements for the Strategy to track progress and develop an outcomes framework with a wide range of indicators to assess progress. Discussions have already taken place with other Departments in this regard and to identify appropriate data. The Department recognises that the impact of the Strategy is much wider than solely rates of suicide and self harm and is keen to reflect the vast number of outcomes which contribute to improving mental health and wellbeing, suicide prevention and self-harm across Departments

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the park and ride facility in Downpatrick.
answer_text: A suitable site for a Park & Ride at Downpatrick, has been identified within the Down Business Park along the A7 Belfast Road. The project has the potential to provide parking for approximately 400 vehicles. Environmental assessments and detailed designs have been completed. Once a business case has been approved, the Department will begin the formal submission of a planning application. However, progression of this park and ride site will be dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following publication of the March 2025 Samaritans report Lost Futures, whether he will consider cross-departmental suicide prevention budgets so that suicide prevention efforts can address the root causes of suicide.
answer_text: ANSWERI firmly support that suicide prevention is a shared Executive responsibility, reflected in the work of the Executive Working Group on Mental Well-being, Resilience and Suicide Prevention. Following Executive agreement of the 2025-26 Final Budget, all Ministers can consider prioritising funding within their own departmental allocations to address mental well-being and suicide prevention, as well as working with other Ministers to deliver services on a cross-departmental basis, where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when there will be a revised Protect Life 2 Strategy.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Strategy has been extended to 2027 with the option of a further extension to 2029. The new Action Plan and Implementation Plan were agreed subject to funding issues being resolved at the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing Resilience and Suicide Prevention on 3 April. The Department is currently considering the financial implications as some new actions cannot be met within existing budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will ensure that the community and voluntary sector is appropriately resourced to meet its role in suicide prevention.
answer_text: I greatly value the work undertaken by the Community and Voluntary Sector and recognise that effective suicide prevention requires delivery across a range of settings and services. A wide range of existing programmes are delivered through the Community and Voluntary sector and we wish to deliver further programmes with the sector subject to funding being available.Further funding is required to ensure the full implementation of Protect Life 2. This remains very challenging with existing budgets. Work is ongoing to identify funding options for the new Action Plan against this challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with other Departments in relation to embedding suicide prevention into their policies and budgets.
answer_text: There was extensive engagement with other Departments during the development of the new action plan and implementation plan for Protect Life 2. A wide range of actions have been agreed with other Departments to ensure their contribution to the Protect Life 2 Strategy and commitments given to support this work which recognises this is a cross departmental issue. The Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention met on 3 April and agreed the content of the two plans subject to funding and these will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Protect Life 2 will be updated to reflect the socio-economic drivers of suicide, as outlined in the March 2025 Samaritans report Lost Futures.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Strategy is not currently being updated however it has been extended until 2027. The new Action Plan and Implementation Plan will continue to target resources towards areas where there is greatest need. The Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention met on 3 April and agreed the content of the Plans, subject to funding. The cross departmental actions will help to address a range of socio-economic drivers of suicide. The plans are being designed and will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department ensures that suicide prevention funding is targeted geographically and demographically based on suicide rates.
answer_text: One of the aims of the Protect Life 2 Strategy continues to be to ensure suicide prevention services and support are delivered appropriately in deprived areas where suicide and self-harm rates are highest. The PHA implement the Strategy and ensure this targeted resourcing continues where the biggest difference can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount NI Water has spent as a direct result of fires on its land in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The spend associated with fires on NI Water land is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have requested this information and NI Water has confirmed that the overall spend on the protection of the Mournes Drinking Water Protected Area (DWPA) is £660,000 over the last ten years.  This includes risk assessment and risk mitigation for the four key elements of drinking water i.e. drinking water catchment area protection, drinking water treatment, distribution system (including pipework and Service Reservoirs) and the customer tap. The element of that spend which is wholly and directly relating to the prevention of wildfires in the catchment area is not readily available and therefore I have asked NI Water to write to you further, when this information is clarified.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the current wildfires on NI Water land will result in further overspending.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the relevant information. NI Water has advised that the potential costs to mitigate against any outlay that can be attributed to the wildfires is currently unknown and may not be confirmed for some time. These costs will need to be met from NI Water’s 2025/26 budget allocation, once agreed.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to establish a dedicated young patients fund for Northern Ireland to assist families in covering the costs of having a child in hospital.
answer_text: I recognise that the unexpected costs for patients undergoing hospital treatment can add additional financial pressure at an already difficult time. There are existing schemes in place to help support individuals and their dependants in Northern Ireland who need access to healthcare. The Help with Healthcare Costs Scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.  		In the absence of an agreed multi-year budget for health and a significant projected overspend, such a scheme would not be currently affordable and would have to be considered in the future alongside the many other competing funding demands across the Health and Social Care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the main areas of spend by NI Water as a result of fires on its land in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The spend associated with fires on NI Water land is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have requested this information and NI Water has confirmed that the overall spend on the protection of the Mournes Drinking Water Protected Area (DWPA) is £660,000 over the last ten years.  This includes risk assessment and risk mitigation for the four key elements of drinking water i.e. drinking water catchment area protection, drinking water treatment, distribution system (including pipework and Service Reservoirs) and the customer tap. The element of that spend which is wholly and directly relating to the prevention of wildfires in the catchment area is not readily available and therefore I have asked NI Water to write to you further, when this information is clarified.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water will work with external agencies to regenerate areas of its land affected by fires.
answer_text: The management of the regeneration of land effected by fires is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have requested advice from NI Water. It has advised that the NI Water Catchment Management and Water Supply teams have been working with Mourne Heritage Trust (MHT) for over 15 years on habitat and erosion management in its drinking water catchments in the Mournes.  Since April 2023 it has been a core partner in the Forever Mournes partnership with MHT, National Trust and Woodland Trust NI. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to recent correspondence in relation to the petition opposing the development of the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project, whether her meeting with the National Trust to discuss the project has taken place.
answer_text: I met representatives from the National Trust on 7 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to recent correspondence in relation to the petition opposing the development of the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project, to detail any parties who will or may be involved in decision-making, subsequent to the initial decision taken by the council.
answer_text: A decision, initially by Council, on whether the project will proceed to construction will only take place once the Council has obtained a fuller understanding of the costs and the environmental and wider sustainability and feasibility issues. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the total sum of Direct Payments allocated by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to families of children and young people with disabilities in lieu of respite care provision, to detail the amount of Direct Payment funding that was returned unspent to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust currently provides 254 families with Direct Payments to deliver a variety of supports to families with children with Severe Learning Disabilities and or complex medical needs. The information management system for direct payments does not centrally capture if the Direct payments are provided in lieu of Short Break care provision. Similarly, the financial department cannot centrally extrapolate the amount of Direct Payments returned unspent. To establish this data a manual examination of hundreds of files over the past 5 years would be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail, out of the total number of children and young people with disabilities previously resident in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area who were taken into residential care, how many had previously been assessed as being on the edge of care in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Within the past 5 years the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has received 19 children with Severe Learning Disabilities into care. The term edge of care was introduced by the Department through the A Life Deserved Strategy (DoH:2021), however, this field has not been entered into the regional ‘Understandings the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland’ assessment framework. It would be accurate to state that all of those children received into care would, prior to entering care, have been on the edge of care, unless there was an unknown or an acute set of circumstances that necessitated a care admission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children with a disability in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust who were assessed as on the edge of care in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) allocated; and (ii) unallocated cases.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust has not centrally collated this information over the past 5 years.  During February 2024 my Department initiated the completion of monthly data of children with a disability in the community who were at risk of entering care across all Health and Social Care Trusts, however this was not applied retrospectively. In February 2024, there were 10 Children with a Disability who met the definition for being on the Edge of Care in the South Eastern Trust . In March 2025, this number had increased to 13.  These young people are allocated to a Social Worker and are in receipt of services. The South Eastern Trust adheres to the definition of ‘Edge of Care’, from the Department of Health Strategy A Life Deserved Strategy (DoH: 2021). This definition captures children residing in the community at risk of entering the care system.  It also includes children in care who are at risk of going deeper into the care system, such as secure care for example and children in care making the transition from a care placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the staff and financial support that has been provided to Professor Holtham’s independent review of Northern Ireland's level of need.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department spent £2 million of funding, announced in October 2024, on improving services for children with disabilities.
answer_text: In October 2024 I announced £13.1 million of recurrent funding with up to £2m funding available in year, to improve services for Children with Disabilities. This is in acknowledgement of the pressures in this area which were also highlighted in the BBC Spotlight programme “I am not okay.”I have personally met with the families involved in the Spotlight programme on a number of occasions and I have provided them with a commitment that my Department will continue to work with the Health and Social Care Trusts to have measurable improvements in Children with Disability services into the future.To date, my Department has worked with Trusts to assess what was deliverable on the ground within the 2024/25 financial year, and have allocated some £1.38m additional funding to Health and Social Care Trusts for 2024/25. With this funding in 2024/25, the Health and Social Care Trusts have focussed on meeting initial short-term priorities. The funding has been utilised to develop various initiatives across Trusts to improve family and community support services for Children with Disability, enhance short break provision and expand targeted care programmes. Health and Social Care Trusts have now confirmed of the £1.38m funding allocated in the 2024/25 year, this funding has allowed expenditure in the following areas in the order of:£133,000 in respect of residential provision;£86,000 on overnight short breaks;£978,000 on family support including day short breaks;£94,000 on therapeutic and behaviour support services; and £85,000 on fostering short breaks.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23821/22-27, to detail the differences between the £15 million pilot her Department is planning to undertake and the original proposal that was costed at £30 million.
answer_text: My Department’s proposal, submitted to the interim Public Sector Transformation Board, was to fund a pilot project to demonstrate how the provision of sustainable urban drainage could transform the way in which rainwater is managed.  The scalable proposal was based around the concept of storing between approximately 10,000m3 and 20,000m3 of water within a catchment, through a series of different nature-based drainage interventions aimed at reducing flood risk as well as reducing spills from combined sewer overflows.  The £15m allocation will enable approximately 10,000m3 of storage to be provided through the pilot project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure that people who cannot safely self-manage the removal of ear wax have adequate access to professional support in the NHS.

In the first instance, however, patients should always contact their GP, Pharmacist or healthcare professional to seek advice on self-management of ear wax removal.  Patients can then be referred to either primary care or secondary care clinics to have ear wax removed.Unfortunately, as with many services in the HSC, demand in primary and secondary care exceeds capacity resulting in lengthening waiting times for treatment.  My Department continues to optimise all available resources whilst seeking additional funding to improve waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is working to support local community groups who are established, or wish to establish themselves, as community resilience groups in times of adverse weather.
answer_text: It is clear the Community & Voluntary Sector has a role to play in community resilience. My department funds the NI Emergency Preparedness Group, and my officials are currently updating my department’s civil contingency priorities.  This work will explore how community resilience can be enhanced, and the role of a wide range of stakeholders in that approach.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any action their Department has taken to further develop planning for community resilience in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The Executive Office co-ordinates civil contingencies preparedness across the Executive, as per the NI Civil Contingencies Framework, Building Resilience Together.Community resilience is part of the work of the Northern Ireland Emergency Preparedness Group (NIEPG), which is co-chaired by the PSNI and Local Government and oversees the work of the three local community level Emergency Preparedness Groups (EPG). The work of these groups is funded by the Department for Communities as the Department responsible for both local government and the voluntary and community sector.Community resilience will be a matter raised in the multi-agency debrief of Storm Éowyn, which The Executive Office will facilitate, and which is due to commence in the near future.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any action their Department is taking to ensure that local authorities have up-to-date emergency response plans.
answer_text: The Executive Office co-ordinates civil contingencies planning across the Executive, in line with the NI Civil Contingencies Framework – ‘Building Resilience Together.’ The Framework lays out arrangements for effective emergency management for preparing for, responding to and recovering from an emergency across Government, Local Government and responding agencies. The Northern Ireland Emergency Preparedness Group (NIEPG) is jointly chaired by PSNI and Local Government. Its purpose is to ensure that work at local level is in line with strategic direction set out in the Framework or provided by CCG (NI).Whilst the Executive Office provides best practice and guidance in the form of the Framework and contributes to greater planning and exercising expertise via the Civil Contingencies Division Learning & Development strategy, it is for local authorities to ensure they have up-to-date emergency response plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department is taking to improve planning for community resilience in the face of adverse weather events.
answer_text: Community Resilience is a feature of the NI Civil Contingency Framework led by The Executive Office, and my department is an active participant in the delivery of that framework.  My officials are currently reviewing and updating my department’s civil contingency priorities.  This work will explore how community resilience can be enhanced, and the role of a wide range of stakeholders in that approach.
