question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the current funding model for Northern Ireland Water.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will develop a community emergency resilience network that can work with agencies to deliver local support during future storms.
answer_text: The Civil Contingencies Group (Northern Ireland) (CCG (NI)) is the principal strategic civil contingencies preparedness body for the public sector, while the NI Civil Contingencies Framework sets out the NI arrangements for effective emergency management, identifying the processes involved in preparing for, responding to and recovery from an emergency.The recent experience of Storm Eowyn has again evidenced the value of the Voluntary Community Sector (VCS) input to problem solving, intelligence-based targeting of effort and dynamic response. My officials are currently developing my Departments’ Civil Contingencies Framework. This work will explore the policy direction and action needed to scope any form of community-based emergency resilience recognising that all departments have a duty to engage with VCS organisations relevant to their policy remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any legislative requirements to pursue a reduction in speed limits to bring them in line with the new speed limits on rural local roads in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has well established guidance for setting local speed limits in both urban and rural areas and will reduce the speed limits in certain locations where it is considered that there is a need to do so.  Our approach to speed limits in general is something I intend to consider during my time in office along with looking to find ways to reduce the numbers killed each year on our rural roads.  I am aware that the default speed limit on rural local roads with an ‘L’ prefix are changing and my officials will keep in contact with their counterparts in the south on the effectiveness of this change.Under powers conferred by Articles 37 and 38 of the Road Traffic Regulation (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, my Department can apply new speed limits where and when it is considered necessary to do so. Any proposals to reduce existing speed limits are subject to careful assessment and consultation. The legislative requirements obligate my Department to firstly publish a Notice of Intention and a draft Order for consultation, advertised in the local press and on my Department’s website. The statutory consultation period is a minimum of 21 days, providing members of the public with the opportunity to review and comment on the proposals. Officials will assess any objections or representations received, engaging with objectors to address and hopefully resolve issues raised to enable the withdrawal so the objections. In circumstances where objections are not withdrawn, an independent senior official will undertake an assessment in line with established guidance, balancing the issues raised against the needs of the scheme. The decisionmaking process provides for the following options: proceeding with the legislation without recourse to a public inquiry; holding a public inquiry; or withdrawing the proposal. Subject to my approval, the proposed legislation is presented to the Committee for Infrastructure for its consideration and scrutiny and, if content, officials arrange to make the necessary legislation as soon as practicable thereafter, which becomes operational 21 days later.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider lowering speed limits in Northern Ireland to bring them in line with the new speed limits on rural local roads in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has well established guidance for setting local speed limits in both urban and rural areas and will reduce the speed limits in certain locations where it is considered that there is a need to do so.  There are currently no plans to reduce the default speed limit on rural roads, however our approach to speed limits in general is something I intend to consider during my time in office along with looking to find ways to reduce the numbers killed each year on our rural roads.  I am aware that the default speed limit on rural local roads with an ‘L’ prefix are changing in Ireland, and my officials will keep in contact with their counterparts in the south on the effectiveness of this change.Following on from the successful implementation of part-time 20mph speed limits at 216 schools, officials are currently preparing a paper on the use of 20 mph speed limits more widely.  I would also like to see what else can be done to improve our attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but also to make places cleaner and greener and to help those walking, wheeling, and cycling. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour, particularly to speeding. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the relationship between speed limits and road deaths.
answer_text: Research shows that there is a correlation between speed and the frequency and severity of road collisions. Our system of speed limits is used to manage this risk in order to enhance road safety and reduce the likelihood of collisions. Speed limits varying according to the type of vehicle being driven and the nature of the road.   Evidence shows that more than 95% of road deaths are due to human error, with causation factors such as careless or inattentive driving, inappropriate speed for the road or the conditions, and drink and drug driving accounting for most deaths and serious injuries.  NI Road Safety Partnership data available on the NIDirect website: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/ni-road-safety-partnership shows that excess speed was a contributory factor in one in seven of all fatal collisions in 2023.  While excess speed is not the main causation factor for the majority of collisions that result in people being killed on our roads, it clearly has an impact on the severity of those collisions.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour.   As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes, particularly to speed when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Public Health Agency review of health needs in special schools.
answer_text: I am committed to working with colleagues in Health and Education to ensure that those children with additional healthcare needs can safely be supported to attend school and be enabled to meet their full potential. Alongside the Education Minister, I met with a number of Special School Principals and alongside the Chief Nursing Officer I visited some of those schools to explore their concerns about the provision of healthcare services. I was moved and impressed by their commitment to ensuring pupils can access the benefits of a school environment as part of their ongoing development.Following that engagement, my Department’s Chief Nursing Officer commissioned the Public Health Agency to work in partnership with the Education Authority and other key stakeholders to undertake a comprehensive needs assessment to scope the healthcare needs of children attending mainstream and special education schools. Our priority remains ensuring that all children have safe access to education, supported by the necessary clinical support.The complexity of the review requires in depth data gathering and stakeholder engagement which is currently progressing.  Once complete, it will allow us to identify the specific health challenges faced by this population, evaluate the complexity of their needs, and determine the support systems necessary to ensure timely, child-specific care within the school environment.  This will include an assessment of service provision requirements for children with additional healthcare needs in schools across Northern Ireland.  With priority collaboration across Departments and other key stakeholders, including ongoing engagement with the NI Commissioner for Children & Young People, I anticipate that a final report will be delivered to me for consideration by the end of May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the definition of neighbouring land in the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 adequately accounts for the privacy implications of tall structures, such as high-rise buildings and ferris wheels, for residents in the wider area.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the definition of ‘neighbouring land’ provided in The Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015 is adequate for the purposes of neighbour notification of planning applications. In making a planning application there is an onus on the applicant to identify the relevant addresses for neighbour notification purposes. The relevant planning authority in dealing with the application should take all reasonable steps to verify the accuracy of these addresses against the parameters of the scheme and may make any necessary amendments.  Applicants are however not confined just to the established parameters for neighbour notification purposes. In taking account of the nature of the proposed development, its scale and context, applicants can broaden notification to include other occupiers of properties likely to be impacted by a proposed development. This can include but is not restricted to potential loss of light, overshadowing and loss of privacy implications. Importantly, anyone can make a representation in relation to a planning application, and do not necessarily have to be neighbour notified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the non-means tested element of the nursing and midwifery bursary of £430.42 per month is adequate for student nurses who work approximately 36.5 hours per week during placement.
answer_text: For several years now, the ongoing financial pressures facing my Department have made any increase in the general, non means-tested element of the bursary support for nursing and midwifery students, unaffordable.  I am, however, aware of the increasing challenges of the current system. I am therefore exploring with my officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department, including in the nursing and midwifery fields.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider reviewing the non-means tested element of the nursing and midwifery bursary to reflect the increased cost of living since 2012, when it was last reviewed.
answer_text: I am currently exploring with my officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider amending planning legislation to ensure that all residents affected by a development proposal involving a tall structure are notified.
answer_text: I am satisfied that current legislative requirements for notifying neighbours of planning applications as set out in the Planning Act (NI) 2011, and The Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015 are adequate to deal with all types of application, including those proposing tall structures.  In making a planning application there is an onus on the applicant to identify the relevant addresses for neighbour notification purposes. The relevant planning authority in dealing with the application should take all reasonable steps to verify the accuracy of these addresses against the parameters of the scheme and may make any necessary amendments.  Applicants are however not confined just to the established parameters for neighbour notification purposes. In taking account of the nature of the proposed development, its scale and context, applicants can broaden notification to include other occupiers of properties likely to be impacted by a proposed development. Importantly, anyone can make a representation in relation to a planning application, and do not necessarily have to be neighbour notified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the proposed changes to rating policy.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will engage with the UK Government to discuss VAT changes in the Republic of Ireland on food-led hospitality businesses, with a view to mitigating the impact on businesses in Northern Ireland's border counties.
answer_text: My department has repeatedly engaged with the British Treasury on the challenges facing our hospitality and tourism industry, particularly in relation to the high VAT rates faced by businesses here relative to the South. Unfortunately, the British Government, which is responsible for setting VAT rates, has so far been unwilling to address this.My department continues to support the local sector, for example through significant rate support, and has separately commissioned an independent study into the cost of doing business here which will consider the impact of North-South VAT differentials.I will continue to make the case for a reduction in VAT to enable our hospitality and tourism sector to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. I will be raising this issue with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the next Finance Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) meeting later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the £1 million support following Storm Éowyn has been spent.
answer_text: On 25 January, I activated and extended the scope of the Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) to provide financial assistance to councils. SEFA will run from 24 January until the situation is resolved. I have ring-fenced up to £1m to alleviate the impact of Storm Éowyn. As the Scheme is still live, and costs are re-imbursed to council retrospectively, it is not possible to provide a more detailed breakdown at this stage. Circular LG 03/25 Non-Flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance to District Councils – Storm Éowyn sets out the eligible expenditure and is available on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether NIE Networks have an obligation to review or investigate its power cables and lines to ensure they are not being compromised by trees or branches.
answer_text: NIE Networks has a number of statutory requirements within the Electricity (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, The Electricity Safety, Quality And Continuity Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012, as well as requirements withing their Distribution Licence; to ensure the integrity of its network.Annually NIE Networks maintains safety clearances to circa 10,000km of overhead lines (and other equipment) from nearby vegetation.  The cutting cycle has also been adapted to accommodate increased tree growth due to climate change and this is kept under constant review.  Furthermore, around 1,800km of overhead line network is refurbished and replaced with modern specification equipment capable of withstanding minor interruptions, for example, from tree contact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, considering the number of fallen trees in the wake of Storm Éowyn, and given the number of rural households living on unadopted roads, (i) whether he will consider making an exception to assist with clearing unadopted roads that are blocked by fallen trees; and (ii) whether financial assistance will be considered for the repair of related structural damage to unadopted roads and footpaths.
answer_text: My Department continues to address the impact of Storm Éowyn across the adopted road network. Where a request is received for assistance on a private laneway, in regard of a vulnerable person, my Department will seek to work with other bodies to provide that assistance. My Department is not in a position to provide assistance in clearing private laneways, even where the laneways serve multiple properties.The remit of my Department does not extend beyond the adopted road network and indeed the level of funding falls well below that which is required to maintain adopted roads to the desired standard due to years of austerity. Therefore I would not be in a position to allocate funding to the repair of structural damage to unadopted roads, lanes and footpaths.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the instances where his Department has made use of its powers, under the Harbours (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, to give a Trust Port directions of a general or specific nature as to the exercise of its functions, including (i) details of the Trust Port involved; and (ii) details of the issue and the directions given.
answer_text: My department has never used its power under the Harbours (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, to give a designated harbour authority directions of a general or specific nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioned work to establish a regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of why the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust scored worst on urgent referral waiting time targets for 11 out of 12 months between October 2023 and September 2024, with between 93.9 per cent and 100 per cent of patients waiting more than 14 days to be seen.
answer_text: NI waiting time targets state that all urgent breast cancer referrals should be seen within 14 days.  In the quarter ending September 2024, the percentage of patients seen within 14 days in all five HSC Trusts is 30.0% ranging from 5.2% (South Eastern HSC Trust) and 99.7% (Western HSC Trust).  The member for South Down is correct that South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has had the lowest performance against this target between October 2023 and September 2024 however, many Trusts are similarly challenged.  Poor performance across the region is impacted by ongoing consultant vacancies in surgery and radiology and demand capacity gaps.  Recruitment challenges are due to local and UK shortages.  South Eastern HSC Trust has been particularly impacted by vacancies, sickness and maternity leave of consultant surgeons and radiologists during this period.  Another factor impacting the poor performance in South Eastern HSC Trust during this period is the implementation of Encompass in November 2023.  The Trust was the first to ‘go live’ with Encompass which resulted in an expected reduction of activity due to the downturn of clinics in the lead up to go-live and stabilisation of service post go-live.  My Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for breast cancer assessment.  During this period non-recurrent funding was allocated to Trusts to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity. However, given the demand and staffing gaps across the region, securing this capacity can be challenging and some Trusts have been more successful than others in sourcing additional capacity.Further information in relation to actions underway by my Department to address the performance of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is detailed in my answer to your AQW 21398/22-27 for response on 11 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions underway by his Department to address the performance of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in relation to new breast cancer referral waiting times.
answer_text: Poor 14-day breast performance across the region is impacted by ongoing consultant vacancies in surgery and radiology and demand capacity gaps.  Recruitment challenges are due to local and UK shortages.  Trusts are committed to filling medical and other vacancies wherever possible including using international recruitment drives and developing skill mix in line with available evidence.  South Eastern HSC Trust has been particularly impacted by vacancies, sickness and maternity leave of consultant surgeons and radiologists.   My Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for breast cancer assessment.  In 2024/25 non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity.  However, given the demand and staffing gaps across the region, securing this capacity can be challenging and some Trusts have been more successful than others in sourcing additional capacity.  In response to the decline and regional disparities in the breast assessment waiting times my department established a regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment as part of a comprehensive Review of Breast Services.  This initiative aims to enable women to access the earliest available appointment across all Trusts.   The regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment commenced on 8 May 2025. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) is the host Trust for the new regional booking system. This new system will ensure everyone who is referred for breast assessment across the regional experiences a similar wait. Most people will still be seen at their nearest hospital, but if there is an opportunity to be seen earlier, patients may be offered an appointment at any of the acute hospitals. Following assessment, patients are be referred back to their home Trust for treatment if required.Work is also underway to address the backlog of patients waiting and to build capacity within breast services across Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to Action 4 of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan, whether he intends to develop a local action plan for the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: Regional Balance is one of the key priorities in my Department’s Tourism Vision & Action Plan: 10 Year Plan, launched on 15 January 2025.  Tourism NI is in the early stages of developing a Destination Stewardship Framework to sustainably develop tourism destinations across the North. Tourism NI is currently procuring services to support the co-creation of the Destination Stewardship Framework. This process will include consultation with key stakeholders, including the Newry, Mourne & Down District Council, Mourne Heritage Trust and stakeholders in the wider Mournes area. A pilot Destination Stewardship Plan will be proposed, based on one of the tourism destinations identified as part of the Framework development. This pilot destination plan will be selected by the specialist contractor in partnership with Tourism NI, using appropriate research, data and evidence. The pilot plan will inform further destination plans in the coming years.Furthermore, when , Conor Murphy MLA was Finance Minister he commissioned the Innovation Lab  to engage with communities and stakeholders on the long-term future of the Mourne Mountains, with the aim of informing the Executive’s approach going forward.  The research pointed towards the importance of the Mournes and the positive impact it has on health, wellbeing and culture, as well as connecting communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to Action 4 of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan, to identify the destination which will pilot the development of a Destination Stewardship Plan.
answer_text: The Destination Stewardship Plan will include a Stewardship framework and principles to be used as a blueprint to develop tourism destinations sustainably in the future.Tourism NI is currently procuring a suitably qualified and experienced contractor to co-create the Destination Stewardship Framework. The specialist contractor will collaborate with Tourism NI and all councils to gather research, data and evidence to inform the decision on the destination selected for the pilot Destination Stewardship Plan.  Following this process, my Department will be in a position to advise on the identified destination selected for the pilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) who has been commissioned to carry out the independent review of Tourist Accommodation, as referenced in the Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan; (ii) when the review is expected to be complete; and (iii) whether a report will be published.
answer_text: (i) Following a tender process, Team Enterprise & Management Ltd (TEAM) was appointed to carry out the independent review of Visitor Accommodation. (ii) The assessment is expected to be completed by spring 2025.(iii) The full assessment will contain commercially sensitive information from across the sector including visitor accommodation providers. For that reason, the report will not be published in its entirety but my Department will consider in due course what elements can be published to provide the evidence base behind any future announcement of new policy in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the independent review of Tourist Accommodation will consider the impact of tourism accommodation markets on local housing markets.
answer_text: The primary objective of the independent assessment is to consider the future provision of visitor accommodation across the North in order to support the growth of tourism. An assessment of the impact of tourism accommodation markets on local housing markets was outside the scope of this project. However, my officials are engaging with the Department for Communities to support its 'Housing Supply Strategy: A Home for Everyone, 2024 - 2039' wherever possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department’s Enabling Access to Justice reform programme.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to Action 14 of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan, for his assessment of how increasing the number of air routes and seats from key markets can contribute to the linked objective of decarbonisation.
answer_text: As the Department develops an Aviation and Route Development Policy to maximise our air connectivity we are engaging with a range of stakeholders. This includes our airports and the airlines that serve them, to determine how we best balance increased connectivity from our key markets with sustainability concerns.The sustainability of the North’s future air connectivity is hugely important in light of the obligations of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, and air carriers will be expected to reduce or offset carbon emissions, with the interaction of UK and EU wide schemes currently under review. In addition, we are supportive of the strong focus of our local airports on reducing emissions and protecting biodiversity and natural environments as part of their operational strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the funding for Phase 3 of the Review of South East Flooding will be made available.
answer_text: The recently published review of the South East Flooding in Autumn 2023 detailed a number of recommendations for which my Department, and many other multi-agency partners, will be responsible for delivering. Although my Department is not the lead agency for the delivery of all the recommendations from the review, I am content for my Department to take the lead in the coordination and oversight of the implementation of all recommendations through the establishment of a Flooding Review Recommendations Group. The first meeting of this group took place in late November 2024 with the January 2025 meeting deferred due to many participants involved with the recovery phase of Storm Eowyn. The focus at this initial stage is to develop and agree an Action Plan outlining responsibilities and associated timescales needed to meet each recommendation with the anticipation that all recommendations will be addressed within 24 months. Therefore, the budget to take forward agreed actions, either assigned to my Department or those of other stakeholders, have yet to be determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of why the percentage of good jobs in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area has been decreasing since 2023, having previously followed a positive trend.
answer_text: The good jobs measure was designed to monitor three areas which are fundamental to all jobs: a permanent contract; non-zero hour contract; and earning the Real Living Wage or above. The decrease in the percentage of good jobs in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area between 2023 and 2024 is largely due to a reduction in the proportion of people earning at least the real living wage, from 80.1% to 75.7%, as reported from the 2024 Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19081/22-27, to detail (i) whether products for the 21 properties have been delivered as anticipated; (ii) the number of these homes that have since seen works under the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme completed; and (iii) the number of these completed works that were related to properties in Newcastle.
answer_text: My Department’s Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS) remains open to new applications. The grant scheme covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs.   In relation to ongoing applications, I am pleased to advise that the delivery of products for 21 properties as detailed previously, whilst initially delayed, has now been received. Works commenced to fit these measures on properties at the end of January 2025 with 3 homes completed to date. It is anticipated that all properties will be completed in 8-10 weeks. In relation to Newcastle I can confirm that 6 properties are waiting for measures to be fitted with works expected to commence within the next 2-3 weeks. My Department’s contractor will contact each homeowner individually to arrange a suitable date for installation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures planned to address the high frequency and volume of spills recorded in 2023 at the Tullybrannigan Road Combined Sewer Monitored Overflow, Newcastle.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has confirmed that the high number of spills at Tullybrannigan Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO), in 2023, were due to blockages within the downstream sewers and suspected infiltration. A full desilt of the downstream sewers was completed in October 2023 and an infiltration investigation has been initiated. Spills from the Tullybrannigan CSO have reduced as a result of the operational interventions and the 2024 data for this CSO is currently undergoing internal quality assurance checks by the company.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on NI Water's ongoing work to complete modelling of sewage spills in less densely populated areas.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water generates modelled spill information for storm overflows using Drainage Area Plan (DAP) models and this sewage discharge information outlines the frequency and volume of discharges as predicted by its DAP models. The figures are effectively an annual average, based on long-term recorded rainfall data.In May 2024, NI Water published on its website (Storm Overflows) predictive spill data for over 1200 storm overflows across 65 catchments (main cities and large towns), representing 50% of its storm overflows. Modelling work is ongoing to complete DAPs across less densely populated areas in the North, covering  approximately a further 650 storm overflows. NI Water anticipates that by the end of 2027, it will have predictive spill data for assets in catchments with a population of over 1,500.The development of DAP models is not planned on the remaining storm overflows as these are in very low populated areas (less than 1,500 population) and the modelling of smaller catchments is often challenging as a result of low flows and smaller pipework, reducing the accuracy of predictive spill results.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a list of education otherwise than at school settings that have temporarily paused operation in the 2024-25 academic year due to low referral numbers.
answer_text: Two Education Otherwise than At School (EOTAS) settings have temporarily paused operation in the 2024-25 academic year due to low referral numbers. These settings are Thornberry EOTAS and Downpatrick EOTAS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the minimum number of pupils required to keep an education otherwise than at school setting operational.
answer_text: EOTASWhile there is no set minimum number to confirm operational delivery in an EOTAS setting, data is constantly analysed to guide practice and service delivery. If the number of children in a centre over a sustained period is low, and their needs can be supported elsewhere within the same service delivery model, operational delivery within a particular setting may cease to ensure financial sustainability.  Exceptional Teaching ArrangementsThe exceptional teaching arrangements service has four group settings, which depending on the physical building and staffing can accommodate from 9 - 20 children. The minimum number required for a centre to operate is therefore usually 9 -10 children, however there may be occasions when the centre will operate with fewer numbers due to individual pupil circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial support available for public realm improvements in small villages.
answer_text: The Department for Communities regeneration policy focus is on urban settlements with a population of 5,000 or more. As small villages have populations of under 5,000 it sits outside my Department’s urban regeneration policy remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 19267/22-27, when he will answer (i) AQW 17101/22-27; and (ii) AQW 17102/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 17101 was responded to on 10 February 2025 and AQW 17102 was responded to on 7 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14771/22-27, to detail the stakeholders that received a copy of the James Hutton Institute’s draft report into the future of the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: A draft of the James Hutton Institute’s report was made available to stakeholders attending a workshop with my officials at the Tollymore National Outdoor Centre in March 2023 to enable discussion of the main emerging themes from the research. The stakeholders in attendance at this event were: the National Trust, NI Water, DAERA, Sport NI, the Mournes Heritage Trust, Ulster Farmers Union and Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 966/22-27, whether a Minister has been identified to work with Executive colleagues to develop and deliver a Mournes Management Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that over 400 care leavers have become homeless after leaving care in the last five years, to detail any actions his Department is considering to improve transition planning and prevent homelessness for young people leaving care.
answer_text: In seeking to improve transition planning for young people in care and to help prevent homelessness among care leavers, as reflected in the draft Programme for Government, the NIHE is working collaboratively with my Department and other statutory sector partners, to develop a strategic ‘New Foundations’ action plan.  This plan will aim to assist young people leaving care and to prevent homelessness by improving the support and opportunities available to each young person leaving care, recognising that each young person is an individual and will require tailored options to meet their individual needs and aspirations. It will enable all relevant government departments and statutory agencies to act together as the Corporate Family for children and young people preparing to leave care and making the transition to adulthood, in line with the approach set out in the strategy for looked after children, ‘A Life Deserved – ‘Caring’ for Children and Young People in Northern Ireland’.In tandem, my Department is also working with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) in its partnership with Homewards NI—as part of a programme put in place by the Royal Foundation to demonstrate that it is possible to end homelessness—to develop proposals to focus specifically on early support and interventions which will prevent homelessness occurring for young care leavers.  This work is ongoing.In addition, the 2021 Review of Jointly Commissioned Support Accommodation (i.e. accommodation for young people which is commissioned jointly by the NIHE and HSC Trusts) reported that the complexity of needs of young people placed in supported accommodation challenges the capacity of current models of provision to effectively meet the needs of care leavers and suggested that trauma-informed enhanced models of provision are required. Work to address the Review’s recommendations will improve transitional planning, with local planning partnership groups now in place to support collaborative planning by HSC Trusts, NIHE and others of services to care leavers.				Further, my Department is working to progress implementation of The Northern Ireland Framework for Integrated Therapeutic Care (NIFITC) for all settings for looked after and adopted children, and for care leavers.  NIFITC consists of 11 building blocks and there is one which specifically addresses transitioning to adult life. The Framework has a clear focus on collaborative working across sectors and professions and, when implemented, will improve transition planning by supporting young people leaving care to maintain and access appropriate services and build their relational networks as they transition out of care.  Information on the Framework, and the individual building blocks, can be found on the Department of Health’s website at: The Northern Ireland Framework for Integrated Therapeutic Care (NIFITC) for Care Experienced Children and Young People | Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how partnership working between his Department, the Department of Health, Health and Social Care Trusts and the Housing Executive can be enhanced to improve transition planning for young people in care and help prevent homelessness among care leavers.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive continues to support young people leaving care via delivery of shared responsibilities with Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts towards meeting the accommodation needs of 16 and 17 year olds which is set out in legislation and the NIHE / HSC Regional Good Practice Guidance on Meeting the Accommodation and Support Needs of Young People aged 16-21.The Youth Homelessness Action Plan which was launched on 25 November 2024 contains a commitment to review the Regional Good Practice Guidance to ensure that young people leaving care are supported. Work on the delivery of this action has already commenced.  The Housing Executive has facilitated training with the Children’s Law Centre and established dedicated Housing Advisors and Team Leaders in all Areas to aid young people presenting as homeless. These staff work closely in partnership with the relevant HSC Trust to shape the service around the needs of the young person.The Housing Executive has also been working closely with a range of statutory sector partners in the development of an action plan in respect of young people leaving care.Following the review of the Jointly Commissioned Supported Accommodation, Supporting People and Health have established Local Planning Partnership Groups in each HSC Trust, the purpose of which is to address need and seek to improve services and provision to improve outcomes for young people.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Local Housing Allowance freeze for 2025 and the impact on private renters.
answer_text: The increase in Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rate from April 2024 assisted many renters with affordability of rent costs and with cost-of-living challenges more generally. Indeed, some households saw an increase in housing support of over 40 percent, which illustrates the scale of the impact that the 2020 freeze had on those living in the private rented sector. If the LHA rate is frozen again from 1 April 2025, the burden will once again be felt acutely by low-income households who will face a growing shortfall between housing support and housing costs.I wrote to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions on 5 March 2024 requesting that, going forward, Local Housing Allowance rates are considered with a view to maintaining pace with the increases in private sector rents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, as part of the Review of the Housing Selection Scheme, to detail any consideration he is giving to ensure that the system adequately acknowledges the increased risk of homelessness for care leavers and awards housing points to this vulnerable group accordingly.
answer_text: The fundamental review of social housing allocations made twenty proposals, which are now in the process of being considered and implemented. The review was intended to produce five key outcomes, including: that those in greatest housing need receive priority, with recognition of their time in need; and that there is an improved system for the most vulnerable applicants. I will consider the Housing Executive’s proposals for amendments to the Selection Scheme in line with the intended outcomes of the review

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the Goldliner 215 service from Downpatrick to Belfast has been moved to Adelaide Street, instead of Belfast Grand Central Station, without access to shelter or connecting services; and (ii) whether there are plans to relocate the service to Belfast Grand Central Station in the future.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm the 215 service has been moved out of Europa, now Belfast Grand Central Station. This temporary move is to facilitate the ongoing public realm works for the new station and to prevent the service being caught up in traffic disruption. Translink plan to move this service into the new Belfast Grand Central Station before the end of 2025. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the commitment to multi-year budgets expressed in the Executive's Budget Sustainability Plan, whether he intends to provide multi-year Supporting People budgets with recurrent annual inflationary uplifts to ensure adequate funding for the homelessness sector.
answer_text: In line with the Spending Review outcome for 2025-26, announced on 30 October 2024, the Executive can only set a Budget for the incoming financial year. The funding for this request will be assessed within the resource envelope allocated to my Department by the Executive for 2025/26 alongside other Departmental and Ministerial priorities.  My Department has provided provisional information for Budget 2026-27 and 2027-28 to allow the Executive to consider the longer-term implications of any spending decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the final decision report based on the Equality Impact Assessment budget consultation will be published.
answer_text: A final decision report will be published by the end of January 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether homeowners whose application to the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme was rejected based on the original eligibility rules will be (i) able to reapply; and (ii) proactively informed of the revised rules.
answer_text: My Department’s Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS) continues to remain open to new applications. The grant scheme covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs.   As you will be aware I instructed my officials to review the application process and eligibility criteria for the scheme and I am pleased to confirm that I approved a new revised criteria on 1 October 2024 which will significantly reduce the application process for the homeowner. My officials have been assessing all new applications against this revised criteria which has been published on my Department’s website at:  Homeowner flood protection grant scheme application pack | Department for Infrastructure. For those homeowners whose applications were refused based on the previous criteria, I can confirm that these will be re-assessed against the revised criteria and applicants notified of any change in the status of their application.In relation to ongoing applications and those where the homeowner has paid their 10% contribution, I am advised that a delivery of products for 21 properties is anticipated in the next 2-3 weeks and, whilst a small number of homes may see works completed prior to Christmas, the remainder will be completed as soon as possible in the new year. Since the Autumn 2023 flooding I can confirm that 26 applicants have paid their contributions with 3 properties having measures fitted. It should be noted that some of the contributions paid after October 2023 were for applications received before October 2023 and not related to that flooding event. My officials are in regular discussion with both the contractor and homeowners to ensure that materials are ordered and fitted as efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to deliver the implementation of targeted lung cancer screening in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The former Minister of Health, Minister Swann, accepted the recommendation by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for asymptomatic people with a history of smoking, aged between 55-74 years old, in tandem with the enhanced integration of smoking cessation services.We are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  We will be working with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to establish the resources required and the likely timeline. This will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. This work will require input and collaboration from multiple partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of public liability claims for personal injury (i) received; and (ii) concluded in each of the last five years, broken down by road user type.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online or via the at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20192020 Table 1 in the above link provides details of (i) the number of public liability personal injury claims received during the last five full financial years.  However, please note that although the majority of personal injury claims are from pedestrians, my Department cannot disaggregate between personal injury claims from pedestrians, cyclists or motorists.Table 4 in the above link provides details of (ii) the number of public liability personal injury claims concluded during the last five full financial years.  Again, please be cognisant of the above caveat regarding the disaggregation of road users.I would, however, advise the member and other members using the above link to be cognisant of the other caveats noted in respect to the information provided on roads related public liability claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the effectiveness of roadside diesel particulate testing, based on insights gathered from the trial of test equipment completed earlier this year.
answer_text: The effectiveness of roadside diesel particulate testing has already been established and is guided by EU recommendation C (2023) 1796 on PN testing and other industry best practice guidance. The purpose of the trial conducted by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) was to determine the practical impact that such a test may have on its testing environment and service delivery model. The trial demonstrated that particulate testing equipment could be integrated into DVA’s existing vehicle testing model and is suitable for deployment at the roadside.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of joint Driver and Vehicle Agency and PSNI road safety operations that were carried out since the introduction, in 2024, of calibrated sound meters to assist with the enforcement of noisy and nuisance vehicle noise emissions at the roadside.
answer_text: From 12 June to 17 November 2024, the Driver and Vehicle Agency has carried out eight joint road safety operations with PSNI where calibrated noise meters were used to measure noise emissions from vehicles at the roadside.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans in relation to the roll-out of roadside diesel particulate testing in the future.
answer_text: A Consultation Report on the proposal to introduce diesel particulate testing during MOT testing and roadside spot checks in the north was published on 19 November 2024 and can be viewed at the following link: Consultation Report - Changing the exhaust emissions test for light vehicles with a modern diesel engine Following this report, my officials are preparing recommendations on the next steps for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of staff in his Department currently working on unadopted developments.
answer_text: There are currently 20.6 Full Time Equivalent Staff working on unadopted developments across my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) unadopted housing developments for which no bond is available; and (ii) housing developments for which the available bond is insufficient to bring the development up to an adoptable standard.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records for unbonded sites as they are unlikely to have been determined during the planning process nor determined under the Private Streets Order. Unfortunately, the number of housing developments for which the available bond is insufficient to bring the development up to an adoptable standard is not easily obtainable from the Departments database.  However, I can confirm that there are 379 development sites where 80% of the houses are occupied but the site remains unadopted for more than 5 years.  Enforcement has not progressed on the vast majority of these sites as the available bond is insufficient to bring the development up to an adoptable standard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's efforts to ensure there is no suspension of maternity services at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: Officials have recently been made aware that in recent months Southern Health and Social Care Trust have had to put in place diverts from Daisy Hill Hospital to Craigavon Area Hospital due to workforce challenges. An early alert notification was made to the Department on 14th October, however the Permanent Secretary had discussions with both the Trusts’ Chair and Chief Executive prior to this which highlighted the significant pressures the Trust are currently facing in relation to Obstetrics and Gynaecology. My officials have had ongoing discussions with Southern HSC Trust which commenced on 10th October to seek:An assessment of the situation;A summary of Trust efforts to maintain the current service to date;An update of any engagement (internal and external) on the issue in recent weeks;Options under consideration and an assessment of viability or otherwise, including any external support which would assist; Officials also coordinated a regional meeting with other Trusts to highlight the issues Daisy Hill are currently facing and to ascertain if any support may be forthcoming.Officials are now receiving a daily situation report from Southern Trust which provides an update on staffing levels, escalation status and activity levels across Daisy Hill Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital.  They also continue to meet with the Trust regularly to discuss the Trust’s medium to long term plans to stabilise the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact of 163 vacancies in his Department's Water and Departmental Delivery Group.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's proposals to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to granting permitted development rights for all rooftop solar panels, as is the case in Scotland.
answer_text: I am aware that Scotland has relaxed their permitted development rights for solar panels on non-domestic buildings.Part 37 of the Schedule to the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 (the GPDO) sets out the permitted development rights for the installation of non-domestic microgeneration equipment. Class A provides permitted development rights for the installation, alteration or replacement of solar PV or solar thermal equipment on a building other than a dwellinghouse or block of flats, subject to certain conditions and limitations, without requiring a planning application.I have currently no plans to review these permitted development rights at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government to address the fact that properties built after 2009 are excluded from the UK Flood Re scheme.
answer_text: The Department engaged with the UK Government when the Flood Re scheme was being established in 2014 to ensure that the North was included.  The 2009 criteria was set by the UK Government and reflected the fact that by 2009 planning policy and flood mapping was available to discourage development in flood plains.  There is ongoing engagement between my Department and DEFRA on the Flood Re scheme as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has investigated the feasibility of improving the surface drain systems in Newcastle to allow surface water discharge when the levels in the Shimna River and Burren River are high, as recommended in the Review of Flooding in South East report; and (ii) to detail the outcome of any such investigations and any resulting plans for implementation.
answer_text: The recently published review of the South East Flooding in Autumn 2023 detailed a number of recommendations for which my Department, and many other multi-agency partners, will be responsible for delivering. This includes Recommendation B2 to investigate the feasibility of improving the surface drain systems within Newcastle taking account of the need to allow surface water discharge when the levels in Shimna and Burren rivers are high.Although my Department is not the lead agency for the delivery of all the recommendations from the review, I am content for my Department to take the lead in the coordination and oversight of the implementation of all recommendations through the establishment of a Flooding Review Recommendations Group. This will ensure a centralised approach with effective communication and the continuation of the good working relationships already developed during the flooding review itself. This group is currently developing terms of reference to provide the necessary framework for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the number of times the Flood Strategy Steering Group has met since October 2023; and (ii) to provide the minutes of these meetings.
answer_text: 'The Flood Strategy Steering Group (FSSG), which is chaired by my Rivers Directorate, is a high level group which meets to discuss policy and strategy around flood risk management. In 2024 the group met on 29 February 2024, 27 June 2024 and recently on 16 October 2024. Minutes from the February and June meetings have been deposited in the Assembly Library. The October minutes are still being agreed and will be deposited in the Assembly Library once finalised. You will note that some information has been redacted, i.e. policy work still in development, or the attendees names from different agencies outside of my Department, whose permission could not be sought in the time allowed to respond to your request.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of funding delivered directly to businesses via the various flood support schemes, administered through Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, since the flooding occurred on 26 October 2023.
answer_text: The Flood Schemes are being administered and delivered by the Councils impacted by the flooding.  I would advise that such queries are directed towards the Councils involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's decision in relation to the provision of a Christmas Goodwill Grant in 2024.
answer_text: Officials are currently preparing a business case for consideration should funding be available.	My Department has undertaken an equality impact assessment on its Budget.  Informed by responses received, and any further Budget allocations received in October Monitoring, the final report is expected to publish in November, at which point I expect to be able to finalise budget allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any further plans to regenerate Downpatrick town centre following the 2023 autumn flooding.
answer_text: My department is currently working closely with Newry Mourne and Down District Council and the Downpatrick Regeneration Working Group to develop and deliver, a range of regeneration schemes in Downpatrick town centre, including:New Skate Park & Pump Track at Dunleath Park;Upgrading of Street Lighting in English Street;Dismantlement of 39 Irish Street;Resurfacing of Irish Street;Development of a Strategic Masterplan for Irish Street  Environmental Scheme in Lynn Doyle Place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for details of the pilot programme in the draft Programme for Government that will support young people leaving care and help to prevent this group from the risk of homelessness.
answer_text: My officials are actively engaging with the Housing Executive and other statutory partners to scope out potential service improvements for young people leaving care. However, the final details of the pilot programme have yet to be agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to address reports that a third of those who are homeless in Northern Ireland are under 18 years of age.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:“The Housing Executive’s approach to supporting households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness, including those homeless households who have children under the age of 18, is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf.The accompanying Year 3 Action plan which can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/ed2fb456-411c-409d-90dd-93d5fb387b09/Homelessness-Strategy-Year-3-Action-Plan.pdf  contains a commitment that the Housing Executive ‘…will publish our Youth Homelessness Action Plan and we will work with key partners to implement the actions outlined within.’The Youth Homelessness Action Plan aims to provide a coordinated response to ensure the Housing Executive addresses the challenges posed by youth homelessness with a focus on prevention, support and accommodation options. The Housing Executive’s Youth Homelessness Action Plan is due to be published in November 2024. While the Youth Homelessness Action Plan has yet to be published, delivery on the 18 actions, within it, has commenced.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to reallocate surplus funds from the (i) Enhanced Flood Support Scheme; and (ii) hardship scheme in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area.
answer_text: There was no specific amount of funding set aside for Newry Mourne and Down District Council (NMDDC).  When coming into office, Conor Murphy agreed to provide up to £10 million to fund three schemes that provided additional support to businesses impacted by the severe flooding in November 2004, i.e. the Enhanced flood Support Scheme (EFSS) and two hardship funds. Whilst it was estimated that the schemes would cost up to £10 million, the final cost of delivering the schemes was less than anticipated.  Conor had asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments, but no realistic deliverable options were identified given the time frame available. However, before leaving office, Conor asked officials to explore options for implementing a more permanent flood support scheme for businesses similar to schemes operating in Britain.  In recognition of the increased number of flood incidents over the past few years, he also felt it would be an opportune time to include a review of support for everyone impacted by flooding, including residents, sports clubs etc.  He, therefore, instructed officials to establish a cross departmental group to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding. A terms of reference for the group is currently being drafted.

answer_text: The Flood Schemes are being administered and delivered by the Councils impacted by the flooding.  I would advise that such queries are directed towards the Councils involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans he has to make use of the 20,000 vacant properties to address the shortage of housing supply.
answer_text: Tackling long term vacant properties and creating opportunities for town or city-centre living plays an important role in regenerating towns and cities, supporting places to be vibrant and sustainable, where people want to live, work and visit.I have been working on a Housing Supply Strategy to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. This includes consideration of how we can make best use of existing buildings to provide much need housing. I am currently finalising this Strategy and intend to bring it to the Executive for consideration shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the reasons why the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan has not yet been published; and (ii) the timeframe for its publication.
answer_text: My Department published the draft Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) for public consultation and plans to publish an updated OREAP and Government response in the coming months. 		The Department and our key stakeholders continue to work collaboratively towards delivery of the actions as outlined within the draft OREAP. 		Offshore renewable energy will have a key role to play in achieving net zero and we remain focused on delivering our offshore ambitions from 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the reasons why the consultation on an offshore Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulations Assessment has not yet been published; and (ii) the timeframe for its publication.
answer_text: The consultation on the Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulations Assessment of Offshore Renewable Energy will be published when all spatial matters are resolved. The process being worked through now will have benefits for the future and ensure a sustainable approach for offshore renewable energy development in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the work of her Department's Innovation Lab, alongside the James Hutton Institute, to develop the long-term sustainable future of the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: The main role of my Department in relation to the Mourne Mountains was to carry out research to identify key issues surrounding its long-term future that need to be addressed.The research pointed towards the importance of the Mournes and the positive impact it has on health, wellbeing and culture, as well as connecting communities. It also highlighted a number of issues such as a disconnected environmental approach; the negative impacts of tourism; overburdened car parks; lack of alternative transport; and poor roads, paths and facilities.The work identified that many of these issues cut across several Government Departments. An initial priority therefore is the need to identify an Executive Minister that would work with Executive colleagues to develop and deliver a Mournes Management Plan.Given its role in the Mournes area, my officials are engaging with colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) in the first instance regarding the issues raised through this work and, in particular, the ownership of these within the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any commitments he has been given by the UK Government in relation to Great British Energy investment in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I met with Secretary of State Ed Miliband in July and emphasised my energy priorities for the Economic Vision.  I put to him my ambition to realise self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy here and I gained his agreement that GB Energy can and must also support decarbonisation in the North of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a report has been compiled as a result of the James Hutton Institute research into the management of the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response provided in AQW 14771/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether stakeholders who participated in the James Hutton Institute research into the management of the Mourne Mountains will have an opportunity to see the findings.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response provided in AQW 14771/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a report will be published as a result of the James Hutton Institute research into the management of the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response provided in AQW 14771/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the James Hutton Institute has completed its research into the management of the Mourne Mountains.
answer_text: The James Hutton Institute carried out research with individuals and organisations in the area as part of a wider work programme looking at the future of the Mourne Mountains. A report, setting out the findings from the research, was completed in March 2023. Although the report has not yet been published, a draft was shared with a number of stakeholders at the time as part of the research process.My officials are currently engaging with colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) regarding the issues raised through the work and, in particular, ownership of these within the Executive. Publication of the research can then be considered by the relevant department(s) that takes on any such role.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any efforts his Department has made to secure the necessary ring-fenced funding to develop a Flood Forecasting Centre, included in the the Budget 2024-25 Equality Impact Assessment Public Consultation; and (ii) any response there has been in relation to this.
answer_text: Currently due to budgetary constraints my Department is operating a limited flood risk management service with no provision to fund a Flood Forecasting Centre. My Department submitted a transformation bid for the delivery of a Flood Forecasting Centre. As this is a digital bid, it is being considered as part of a digital landscape review, the outcome of which is not expected until later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department proposes to mitigate the equality implications resulting from the expected impact of reduced NI Water on social housing development, given the assessment provided in the Capital Budget 2024-25 Equality Screening.
answer_text: I have provided NI Water with a total budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion in 2024-25, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.NI Water is assisting developers with development planning, including those involved in the development of social housing, by encouraging early engagement, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, and to arrange for initial discussions on wastewater issues at the beginning of the process, to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage.I can also advise that a Development Constraints Project Team has been put in place, within NI Water’s Developer Services function, to help alleviate capacity constraints, and that NI Water endeavours to facilitate development, wherever possible.We all want to see a sustainable water and wastewater infrastructure which benefits everyone across our society; therefore, I will continue to raise the need for NI Water, to have secure and sustainable funding, with the NI Executive and the British Government, to help deliver the necessary infrastructure to enable the building of homes for all our people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to voluntary sector mental health charities taking referrals from statutory agencies but not receiving any statutory funding, for his assessment of this funding model.
answer_text: In delivering on Actions 17, 31 and 32 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, the Department intends to undertake a review of the scope, scale and capacity of the Community & Voluntary (C&V) sector during 2024/25. This review will aim to identify and map the current levels of funding (both government and non-government) of the sector, including impact and outcomes being delivered across the range of mental health services provided.  This work will include recommendations on optimum approaches for utilising existing funding and resources to best effect in the delivery of mental health priorities, including how current commissioning and funding arrangements may be strengthened.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to replace the Mental Health Support Fund.
answer_text: The Mental Health Support Fund was established using unspent Covid-19 monies to help equip and enable our local charity sector to continue to provide vital mental health services in our communities. The fund was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. All monies allocated to the Mental Health Support Fund have now been exhausted and it has now ended. The 2024-25 Resource budget for the Department, regrettably passed by the NI Assembly and supported by you, represented a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to expenditure in 2023-24. Health and Social Care (HSC) is facing a significant funding shortfall. The financial position therefore remains very challenging, and work continues across the HSC system to deliver the maximum possible savings and efficiencies. Therefore, there is currently no capacity within the current budget to extend or replace the fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department is working to reduce its carbon footprint.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 - AQO 745/22-27ANSWERMy Department is committed to delivering its responsibilities under the Climate Change (NI) Act 2022 and in supporting DAERA in the development of the first NI Climate Action Plan. While the Department of Justice is not a sectoral lead under the Act we aim to contribute to the sectors of buildings, transport and energy.Our 2023 Outline Sustainable Strategy sets out our goals and supporting actions to reduce our carbon footprint. For example, we have established 2021/22 as our baseline year for measuring our energy consumption and reducing our reliance on fossil fuels. We have an active information programme in place to encourage behavioural change with our staff, both in the workplace and at home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the multi-agency review of the autumn flooding in the south east, to provide a timeframe for when the final report will be published.
answer_text: The final report on the multi- agency review of Autumn flooding in the South East will be published in  July 2024.Some of the measures evident from the report, and from the open day feedback, are around communication in respect of flood reporting, insurance, financial assistance and working with communities in areas of flood risk. My Department, along with other multi-agency partners, will seek to address those areas of work which can be acted upon reasonably quickly. In addition, my Department, as the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to significant flooding, will continue to work with partner organisations to ensure that so far as possible pre-planned arrangements are in place to help mitigate against any future flooding events.Considerations around the release of the report later this month to NI Executive colleagues and the wider presentation and communication of this report are ongoing.The joint flooding review group are currently working to establish governance and responsibility for delivery of the recommendations in the report, so I am unable to provide specific information on the detail you request at this time.
